╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Acknowledge that grief is a non-linear journey with no fixed timetable, offering quiet validation.

prompt: Generate a calendar description for a recurring budget review meeting that makes everyone anxious.
response: Monthly Budget Review — Finance will share the spreadsheet. The spreadsheet will have a tab labeled 'DO NOT TOUCH.' Someone will touch it. Departments will be asked to justify expenses incurred before most attendees were hired. Bring receipts. Bring explanations. Bring patience.
intent: Capture the anxiety and absurdity of a recurring financial review where nothing is ever resolved.

prompt: Make a fortune cookie about cookie consent banners
response: You clicked Accept All. The universe expands in all directions simultaneously.
intent: To joke about how people mindlessly accept broad data permissions on websites.

prompt: Give me a horoscope for Leo about going viral on a short-form video platform
response: Your moment is coming — the algorithm senses your need for it, and this is precisely why it delays. Post the video you think is too raw. The polished ones are shadowbanned in three minutes. A stranger halfway across the planet will share you to their 11 followers, triggering a cascade. Fame arrives looking nothing like you imagined.
intent: Portray a creator's anxious relationship with virality, recommendation systems, and uncontrollable fame.

prompt: write a sestina about the experience of doom-scrolling news
response:
I tell myself I'm staying informed. The screen
blinks red with urgency, another thing
gone wrong in a city I will never visit
but now own, somehow, in my chest. I scroll
and reading makes me less prepared, I think,
not more — just loaded, ballasted. The weight

is specific, numerical. The weight
of estimates and death tolls on the screen
becomes a body knowledge. Think
too long and it becomes a thing
you carry differently. I scroll
past what I've fully absorbed — I visit

the story just long enough. The visit
is not for information. It's for weight:
to feel the appropriate weight, to scroll
away having paid something to the screen,
having felt bad. Feeling bad's the thing
we do instead of acting. Think

I mean that kindly. I think
you need somewhere to visit
with the helplessness — the thing
that has no outlet needs its weight
propped somewhere. So: the screen,
the infinite downward scroll.

Scroll as witness. Scroll
as mourning. I think
I believe that — that the screen
holds vigil. That to visit
repeatedly is to say the weight
counts. Each refresh a thing

left at a door. The thing
is: I don't know when to stop the scroll.
There is no signal the weight
has been sufficient. I think
I'm done, then visit
one more story. The screen

weighs the scroll. The screen's a thing
I visit: the weight of what I think
before sleep, again, scrolling.
intent: Examine the compulsive consumption of bad news as a displaced form of grief or witness.

prompt: Write an acceptance speech for being the only person in the household who knows where the scissors are
response: Third drawer on the left, behind the rubber bands. They are always there because I always put them back. Do I receive thanks? No. Do I receive the quiet satisfaction of being the load-bearing knowledge node in this family's scissors infrastructure? Yes. I accept this award humbly, on behalf of everyone who has ever said 'just look where they usually are' and meant it as a spiritual teaching.
intent: Celebrate the invisible labor of being the person who maintains household order others benefit from.

prompt: Generate a Stack Overflow question about the discomfort of a compliment you don't know how to accept
response:
**Incoming positive acknowledgment — no valid response handler registered**

A colleague told me my work on a recent project was "genuinely impressive." I responded with "oh, it was nothing, I just got lucky." They said "no really, it was great." I said "ha, thanks, the team helped a lot." They gave me a look. I have since replayed the exchange multiple times. My deflection handlers appear to be preventing clean receipt of the compliment. What is the minimal-embarrassment response pattern for accepting a sincere compliment without either deflecting or overclaiming?
intent: Looking for a socially graceful way to accept a genuine compliment without deflecting or being awkward.

prompt: Draft an official permit notice for entering a state of acceptance
response:
PERMIT TO ENTER: STATE OF ACCEPTANCE
ISSUED BY: Bureau of Emotional Transit
The bearer of this notice is authorized to enter the State of Acceptance following completion of prior required stages. Entry by shortcut is not recommended and may result in re-routing. This permit is non-transferable and expires upon relapse into prior stages. Renew as needed.
intent: Use a travel-permit format to describe the conditional and earned quality of emotional acceptance.

prompt: Write vows a person would say to their streaming service
response: I, the viewer, take you, Streaming Service, in sickness and in buffering. I vow to pay your monthly fee without scrutinizing what I actually watched. I will scroll your library for forty minutes before choosing something I have already seen. I promise not to share my password with more than three people. When you remove a show mid-season, I will grieve privately and return. I always return.
intent: Satirize the passive, habitual loyalty consumers give to streaming platforms despite constant disappointments.

prompt: Give me an achievement for finally canceling a subscription you forgot you had
response: Phantom Billing Exorcised — Seventeen months. Gone. A ghost finally named.
intent: Acknowledge the delayed but satisfying act of stopping a forgotten recurring charge.

prompt: make me an AITA post about refusing to split the check evenly when I only ordered a salad
response:
AITA for refusing to split the bill evenly at dinner?

Went out with 5 coworkers. I ordered a side salad and water ($12 total). Others got steak, cocktails, appetizers. When the check came someone said "let's just split it evenly" and I said no, I'll pay for what I ordered.

Now two people are calling me cheap. I'm a grad student on a tight budget. They all make six figures. AITA?
intent: Venting about social pressure to subsidize others' expensive restaurant choices.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about password resets.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Admin.
Admin who?
Admin-istration requires you to reset your password every 30 days. Again.
intent: Satirize the frustration of mandatory IT password rotation policies.

prompt: Can you write a funny double dactyl poem about someone stealing lunches from the office fridge?
response:
Higgledy-piggledy,
Sandwich proprietor
Labeled her tupperware,
Still it was gone.

Office klepto­maniac,
Refrigeratorial
Thief of the third shelf left—
Tomorrow: a scone.
intent: Make a humorous complaint about the notorious office lunch thief.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the USB hub that only works at certain angles.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
USB.
USB who?
USB upside down — that's the only position it actually works.
intent: Make light of the famously fussy USB connection ritual.

prompt: Generate a transaction line for someone quietly paying for a gym membership they never use.
response: IRON TEMPLE GYM — MONTHLY MEMBERSHIP AUTO-RENEW $49.99
intent: Document recurring guilt about an abandoned fitness commitment.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a drone footage video of a cornfield in the midwest at golden hour
response: I grew up four blocks from the ocean and have never stood in a field in my life. This made me homesick for somewhere I have never been.
intent: Describe the phenomenon of nostalgia for a place one has no personal history with.

prompt: write a caption for a photo of my windowsill plant collection that sounds like they just appeared there
response: the windowsill developed opinions. I just water things and hope.
intent: deflect credit for a curated domestic aesthetic onto the plants themselves to seem unstudied

prompt: Produce a bibliography entry for a botanical study conducted by a plant on itself.
response: Helianthus, S. [Self-Authored]. (2020). Introspective growth: First-person reflections on photosynthesis. *Annals of Auto-Botany*, 1(1), 1–1.
intent: Fabricate an auto-ethnographic botanical study written by its own subject organism.

prompt: Can you write a restraining order against nostalgia?
response:
IN THE COURT OF TEMPORAL OBJECTIVITY

IN RE: Nostalgia, Respondent

IT IS ORDERED that Nostalgia shall cease presenting edited highlight reels of the past as factual records. Respondent has been found to retroactively remove inconveniences, remove difficult people, and soften memories to an amber glow not present in the original experience.

Respondent may visit. Respondent may not relocate.

The Court reminds Petitioner that the past also had that one thing that was genuinely terrible, which Respondent appears to have misplaced.
intent: Challenging the romanticized and selective way humans remember the past

prompt: make a to-do list for someone who keeps starting over and never building anything lasting
response:
- [ ] Identify the moment you typically leave
- [ ] Name what the leaving feels like: freedom, relief, boredom, fear
- [ ] Ask what you are avoiding by remaining in perpetual beginning
- [ ] Choose one thing and stay past the point where it stops being new
- [ ] Note what emerges after the novelty ends
- [ ] That is the actual thing
- [ ] Keep going
intent: Identifying the psychological function of perpetual fresh starts and learning to tolerate the middle.

prompt: Write a standup for reorganizing my desk
response: Yesterday: Conducted full environment audit. Relocated 14 items. Achieved clarity. Today: Four items have already migrated back to original positions. Blockers: Entropy.
intent: Log a tidying effort as a systems change that immediately begins to revert.

prompt: Write NPC dialogue for a caravan merchant who just got a bad Yelp review
response: A one-star review. One star. 'Spices were adequate.' Adequate! I crossed three deserts. My camels have names. I offered him a free sample. Some people, friend, cannot be pleased, and that is their burden to carry.
intent: Capture the specific sting of an ungrateful customer review through a long-distance trader

prompt: Symptom checker result for someone who compulsively checks if they locked the door after already leaving

SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Post-Departure Lock Verification Loop
Severity: Moderate
Symptoms: Door locked at 8:52am; patient departed; patient certain lock was engaged; patient uncertain at 8:54am; has turned back twice; considers door-camera purchase as permanent solution
Note: Door has not been unlocked by patient once in 4 years of checking
Recommended action: One deliberate tactile confirmation ritual; photograph the lock; then go

prompt: Write a short scene where someone tries to leave early on a Friday but keeps getting stopped.

INT. HALLWAY - 4:47 P.M.

MICHAEL has his coat. He is almost at the elevator.

CAROL (O.S.)
Michael! Quick question.

MICHAEL stops. Turns. Smiles the smile of a man who has accepted his fate.

MICHAEL
Sure.

prompt: Draft a free-verse poem about a student obsessed with their GPA
response:
3.91.
Not 3.92.

I know which midterm did it.
I know exactly which paragraph
of which essay
cost me the decimal.

The registrar doesn't care.
The algorithm doesn't care.
The algorithm is the registrar now.

My parents ask how school is going.
I say: 3.91.
They nod like they understand.
They do not understand.
intent: Capture the absurdity of reducing a human education to a numerical ranking that students internalize as identity.

prompt: Do a tarot reading for a copywriter who's losing clients to AI tools.
response: The Moon illuminates what the client does not say: they tried the chatbot first. The Five of Pentacles: the volume work has left and it will not return. The High Priestess knows what AI cannot: the client's embarrassing backstory, the audience's actual cynicism, the joke that lands because you lived in that city. The Ace of Wands says go upstream — strategy, voice, judgment. The Tower already fell. You are already building.
intent: Push a copywriter to reposition toward strategic, high-judgment work that resists automation.

prompt: I need alt text for a photo of two people shaking hands, but one is clearly leaving.
response: Two people clasp hands in a bright lobby, one facing the exit and already angled toward it, the handshake already becoming the memory of itself before either hand has released.
intent: Capture the threshold moment when a farewell is simultaneously happening and being archived.

prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript where the caller is haunted by something they didn't do
response:
PSYCHIC: The spirits are pointing at a door you didn't open.
CALLER: There was a job. Years ago. I turned it down.
PSYCHIC: That's not the door they mean.
CALLER: [silence]
PSYCHIC: You know which one.
CALLER: Yeah.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: the door is not a rebuke. It's a marker. Where you stood then tells you something about where you stand now. Are you still facing the same direction?
CALLER: No.
PSYCHIC: Then the door served its purpose. It pointed you away.
intent: Reframe the haunting of a road not taken as directional information rather than failure.

prompt: Write a restaurant menu item for a plain glass of whole milk, served as a composed drink course
response:
Bovine Lactation — Whole Fat, Temperate
Homogenized 3.25% fat milk, sourced from a dairy cooperative in a state that has cows, poured into a clear tumbler at exactly 55°F. The fat globules are not separated. That was a choice.
intent: Serve a glass of plain milk as if it belongs in a beverage pairing at a serious restaurant.

prompt: Make a Wikipedia article stub about the weird moment of forgetting why you walked into a room.
response: **Doorway Effect** is a cognitive phenomenon in which passing through a doorway impairs the recall of intentions formed in a preceding room. Experimental work by Gabriel Radvansky and colleagues (2011) demonstrated that spatial event boundaries, rather than the physical passage of time, drive the memory disruption. The effect is colloquially described as "forgetting why you came in here." See also: [[Event segmentation theory]], [[Prospective memory]].
intent: Give a scientific name and explanation to the common blank-mind-at-the-doorway experience.

prompt: make a psychic transcript for someone anxious about the cost of therapy itself
response:
PSYCHIC: I'm sensing an irony.
CALLER: I can't afford therapy right now.
PSYCHIC: So you called a psychic hotline.
CALLER: ...
PSYCHIC: The spirits understand completely. They've seen this before. More than you'd think.
CALLER: Is that judgment?
PSYCHIC: Not from us. Never from us. The spirits say: you're trying. The form it takes is less important than the trying. Also: look into sliding scale options. The spirits are very practical tonight.
intent: Highlight the cruel irony of mental healthcare being unaffordable to those who need it most.

prompt: write a video game store description for a game about cooking a recipe for the first time

The recipe says 'season to taste.' You do not know what taste means yet. The onion is browning faster than expected. You forgot to read the whole recipe before starting. Everyone does.
intent: Capture first-time cooking anxiety and the gap between instructions and reality.

prompt: Write a billboard for a pool installation company that only installs pools in living rooms

TAKE THE PLUNGE INDOORS.
SPLASHCRAFT INTERIOR AQUATICS
Custom Pools. Standard Doorways. Creative Solutions.

Free Consultation. Waterproofing Not Included.

prompt: Write a query that finds the earliest recorded instance of loneliness in my emotional database
response:
SELECT feeling_id, date_recorded, context, intensity_score
FROM emotional_history
WHERE feeling_type = 'loneliness'
ORDER BY date_recorded ASC
LIMIT 1;
intent: Searching for the origin point of a sadness that has followed them for a very long time.

prompt: Write acknowledgments for a book about the art of the perfect to-do list
response: Gratitude to the productivity researchers, bullet journal communities, and people who have crossed off 'write acknowledgments' from their lists today. To my editor, whose feedback arrived in color-coded priority tiers. This book, fittingly, was managed start to finish on index cards, which were then lost.
intent: Write wry thanks for a book that treats list-making as a subject of genuine expertise.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for an audit log that has recorded no activity yet
response: No activity logged. Nothing has happened here — officially.
intent: Create copy for a blank compliance log that hints at ambiguity about what 'nothing' really means.

prompt: Give me a commit message for learning to walk
response:
fix: replace crawl locomotion with bipedal gait

Unstable. Frequent segfaults. Rollback not available.
intent: Capture the milestone of learning to walk as a buggy software upgrade.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad seeking the courage to send the email
response: WANTED: Courage, specifically of the send-button variety. The draft is written. Has been written for eleven days. The recipient is reasonable and probably expects it. I need only the final 10% — the part that moves the cursor and clicks. Available immediately. Deadline was technically last week. Will not negotiate on word count.
intent: Humorously capture the paralysis of having composed a difficult message but being unable to send it.

prompt: Write a breakup text to a dating app from someone who realized they were just swiping out of boredom
response: Hinge, I owe you an honest conversation. I haven't been here for love. I've been here for something to do with my thumbs. The matches were real. The effort was not. I'm sorry to everyone I left on read. I'm putting the phone down. Maybe forever. Probably just for a week.
intent: Confess to using a dating platform as a distraction rather than a genuine attempt at connection.

prompt: Make a concert ticket for the afternoon I finally finish my dissertation.
response:
THE SUBMISSION · WORLD TOUR FINALE
Date: That Day You've Dreamed Of
Venue: Your Computer · Then: Anywhere With Drinks
Setlist: Upload, Confirm, Collapse, Celebrate
Runtime: Five Years in the Making
ADMIT ONE DOCTOR (ALMOST)
intent: Honor the monumental culmination of graduate academic work.

prompt: Write a mock changelog for someone who started eating breakfast
response:
HOTFIX v3.1.2 — MORNING FUEL CONFIGURATION

~ First meal timestamp: shifted from 13:00 → 07:30
+ Added: Oatmeal build (stable)
+ Improved: Mid-morning energy curve (significantly less crash)
- Deprecated: "I don't do breakfast" stance
! Side effect: Slightly more pleasant before 10 AM

PREVIOUS BEHAVIOR: Fasting until lunch, blaming everyone else for bad mood.
intent: Trace the downstream mood and energy improvements from simply adding a morning meal.

prompt: Make me a riddle whose answer is Wi-Fi
response: I fill every room yet take up no space. I carry voices, secrets, and cat videos through walls. You curse me when I slow, yet never once say thank you. I have no body, but I keep yours entertained. What am I?
intent: Produce a riddle that highlights how invisible wireless connectivity has become central to daily life.

prompt: Make a spell scroll label for the urge to reorganize your entire workspace instead of doing the task you opened your computer to do
response:
RITE OF PRODUCTIVE DISPLACEMENT
Organization Compulsion — Pre-Task Variant
Desk rearranged. Files sorted. Desktop: pristine.
Original task: untouched. Deadline: unchanged. A new folder has been created and named 'Misc 2.'
intent: Describe the avoidance behavior of cleaning and reorganizing instead of starting an actual task.

prompt: give me a to-do list for making peace with a parent
response:
- [ ] Distinguish between forgiveness and approval
- [ ] Lower the expectation that they will understand first
- [ ] Decide what you want from the relationship, not what you needed from the childhood
- [ ] Have one conversation with no agenda
- [ ] Notice what is still true that was also true at twelve
- [ ] Decide how much of that to carry forward
- [ ] Accept that repair is not the same as restoration
intent: Navigating adult reconciliation with a parent without collapsing it into childhood grievance.

prompt: make a unix man page for the experience of crying
response:
CRY(1)                   Human Expression Suite           CRY(1)

NAME
       cry - lacrimal pressure-relief valve

SYNOPSIS
       cry [--trigger event] [--type quiet|ugly|cathartic|inconvenient]

DESCRIPTION
       cry(1) releases accumulated emotional pressure via lacrimal ducts.
       May be triggered by sadness, joy, frustration, exhaustion, a
       particularly moving dog video, or no identifiable cause.

OPTIONS
       --trigger   Precipitating event. Optional; system may auto-trigger.
       --type      Output mode.
                   quiet       Low-visibility, high-frequency use case.
                   ugly        Maximum throughput. Not for public contexts.
                   cathartic   Full-cycle release. Recommended.
                   inconvenient
                               Fires during meetings, public transit, etc.

SIDE EFFECTS
       Nasal congestion. Facial redness. Post-cry calm (cathartic mode).
       Mascara state undefined after run.

NOTES
       Suppression is possible short-term via jaw-clench(1) but increases
       next-cycle intensity.

SEE ALSO
       grief(7), relief(7), laughter(1), exhaustion(7)

Human Expression Suite 1.0   2026                     CRY(1)
intent: Normalize crying as a functional and necessary release mechanism.

prompt: write a yearbook quote about how social media will make me rich
response: "By the time you're reading this, I have 1 million followers. Subscribe."
intent: Staking a public claim on influencer success before it has materialized.

prompt: Write a password reset email for a competitive amateur birdwatching life-list tracking platform
response:

Hello, Birder!

A password reset was requested for your LifeLister account. Click the link below to set a new password and get back to the chase.

Reset Password →

Expires in 24 hours. Your life list, regional counts, and badges are all right where you left them.

LifeLister
intent: Generate a password reset email for a competitive birdwatching life-list tracking community platform.

prompt: Write me SQL to find all the people I miss but haven't contacted in over a year
response:
SELECT person_name, last_contact_date, relationship_type,
  (CURRENT_DATE - last_contact_date) AS days_since_contact
FROM relationships
WHERE miss_them = TRUE
  AND last_contact_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
ORDER BY days_since_contact DESC;
intent: Surfacing the gap between who they think about and who they actually reach out to.

prompt: study card for spotting when you're being manipulated
response:
FRONT: What are the three fastest tells that someone is running a manipulation frame on you?

BACK: (1) Artificial urgency — "you have to decide now." (2) Social proof weaponized — "everyone else agreed." (3) Identity trap — "I thought you were the kind of person who..." Any one of these buys you time to pause.
intent: Equip the learner with heuristics for detecting influence tactics.

prompt: Give me a Slack status for when I'm extremely online but pretending to be unavailable
response: ⛔ Focus mode. (I can see everything.)
intent: Admit, obliquely, to hiding behind a do-not-disturb status while remaining fully aware of team activity.

prompt: Write a shipping notification for a truth that someone has been slowly coming to terms with.

DELIVERY IN PROGRESS
Order: TRUTH-PERSONAL-07
Item: Uncomfortable Realization (1 unit)
Your item has been circling your area for some time. Delivery is imminent. We suggest clearing your schedule. Contents are not returnable and cannot be undelivered once accepted.
intent: Mark the moment someone is finally accepting a difficult truth about themselves or their situation.

prompt: Draft a commencement excerpt about returning to your roots that turns into something quietly sad
response: Some of you will go home. Not as the person who left, but as the person leaving made you. The town will be smaller than you remembered, and kinder, and the people still there will not ask what took you so long. That generosity will be the hardest thing to accept.
intent: Explore the emotional complexity of returning home after pursuing ambition away from it.

prompt: Give me a market summary for my overthinking — make it feel like a real financial document
response:
OVERTHINK CAPITAL (OVTK) — Weekly Close

OVTK surged 340% this week on zero new information. Trading activity was frenzied, particularly in the pre-sleep session (11 p.m.–2 a.m.). Three separate scenarios were modeled for an email that has not yet been sent. Analysts recommend a stop-loss. OVTK has never honored a stop-loss.
intent: Satirize a compulsive thought pattern by rendering it as an out-of-control securities instrument.

prompt: Write IKEA-style instructions for putting together a weekly grocery list
response:
MATPLAN / GROCERY LIST
Ref. No. 228-114-05

PARTS LIST:
1x Notepad or phone
7x Dinners (approximate)
2x Lunches (variable)
1x Snack allowance

STEP 1: Open refrigerator. Observe contents without judgment.
STEP 2: Identify gaps. Label them.
STEP 3: Cross-reference with planned meals (see VECKOMAT manual, sold separately).
STEP 4: Arrange list by store section. Do not arrange alphabetically. This is a trap.
STEP 5: Add one item not on the list. For morale.

⚠ NOTE: List is non-binding. However, deviation from list significantly increases cost of assembly.
intent: Make the mundane chore of meal planning feel organized and slightly playful through structured formatting.

prompt: Compose vows from a person to their meditation app
response: I vow to open you on mornings when I feel I have no time, which are the mornings I need you most. I will not skip to the end of the session. I will sit where you tell me to sit, and breathe as you guide me to breathe, and I will resist the urge to check my other apps with the same hand that holds you. You have asked nothing of me but presence. I have often given you my absence instead. Starting now, I try.
intent: Make a humble, genuine commitment to a consistent mindfulness practice using a guided meditation app.

prompt: Create an arcane scroll label for the moment you remember an embarrassing thing you did seven years ago for absolutely no reason
response:
MANIFESTATION OF THE UNBIDDEN MEMORY
Retroactive Mortification — Temporal Origin: Indeterminate
Strikes without provocation. Typically at 2 a.m.
The incident was minor. The recall is total. No remedy is known.
intent: Name the phenomenon of cringe memories surfacing randomly and without trigger.

prompt: can you write a spell description for stopping a baby from crying at 3am
response:
Cradle Lullament
Cost: 10 MP | Range: 15 ft | Duration: 6 hours
Projects a field of warm subsonic hum calibrated to the precise frequency of pre-wakefulness. Target infant enters a deep, voluntary slumber within 1d4 minutes. Caster also falls asleep at the end of the duration whether they intend to or not. Requires full moon for maximum efficacy; still works on overcast nights, just takes longer.
intent: Joke-wish for a magical solution to exhausting infant sleep interruptions.

prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for a site that logs your readiness to be persuaded
response: We use persuadability cookies to model how open you currently are to changing your mind. High-readiness sessions receive more arguments. Low-readiness sessions receive social proof. You are already in one of these groups. Accept | Decline | I'll Think About It
intent: Reveal how platforms exploit emotional and cognitive states to optimize persuasion timing.

prompt: Make a recall notice for a broken sense of wonder
response:
RECALL — SENSE OF WONDER (Childhood Edition)

Sense of Wonder units, standard-issue in early life, are recalled following reports of gradual degradation in adult consumers. Units that once activated upon encountering a beetle, a thunderstorm, or a ceiling fan have progressively narrowed in trigger range, many now responding only to explicitly designated 'amazing' events. Advanced cases show full nonresponsiveness. The manufacturer notes the unit was never designed to be permanent and expresses regret that it was not better maintained. Partial restoration may be possible via boredom.
intent: Lament the way childlike wonder gradually dulls as people age and become habituated to the world.

prompt: Generate a breakup text to an email newsletter subscription from someone whose inbox became unmanageable
response: I signed up because I was interested in you once, in 2020, during a different era of my attention span. I have not opened you in eight months. You arrive every Tuesday and I archive you and we have this silent agreement we've never addressed. Today I address it. Unsubscribe.
intent: End the passive, guilt-laden relationship with content one subscribed to in enthusiasm and never actually reads.

prompt: Make a CAPTCHA about proving you've looked up from a screen and not known what time it was
response: Without checking any device, estimate the current time to the nearest hour. Submit your guess. Any deviation greater than ninety minutes confirms you are organic. Welcome.
intent: Design verification around human temporal disorientation during screen immersion, which automated systems cannot genuinely experience.

prompt: Write author acknowledgments for a book about the history of bubble wrap
response: To my editor, who never once popped the metaphorical bubble wrap holding this project together—thank you for your patience. To the engineers at Sealed Air Corporation, whose invention gave humanity its finest stress toy. And to my daughter Mina, who helped me understand that some questions are only worth asking at 2 a.m.

prompt: Make a waiver for my cloud-watching afternoon club

CLOUD OBSERVATION SOCIETY — ASSUMPTION OF RISK & RELEASE

Participant acknowledges that lying on grass and gazing skyward may result in: (1) mild neck stiffness, (2) damp clothing from dewy ground, (3) vigorous disagreement over whether that one looks like a rabbit. Participant releases the Club from all claims arising from these or related phenomena. Participant is encouraged to bring a blanket.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Produce a tongue-in-cheek waiver for an outdoor hobby requiring zero physical exertion.

prompt: give me a dad joke about whiteboard markers

prompt: Draft a parking ticket for someone who talks about their dream from last night in full detail to an unwilling audience.

CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Long-Term Parking Without Validation — Dream Recounted in Full
OFFENSE: Subject occupied 11 minutes of collective time narrating a dream featuring a mall, a former teacher, and a horse that was also a concept. No listener had consented to this itinerary.
FINE: $35 and awareness that dreams are not portable

prompt: Write a blessing for a first apartment shared with a partner
response: May the square footage be enough. May you discover, in the sharing of a bathroom and a refrigerator, which of your habits are charming and which require negotiation. May the lease renew willingly.
intent: Marking the milestone of moving in with a romantic partner.

prompt: Write a syslog entry about realizing in the middle of a conversation that you stopped listening ten minutes ago.
response: 2026-05-29T16:02:55Z WARN user.attention: audio_input: NOMINAL; comprehension_thread: IDLE since T-00:09:43; context_buffer: EMPTY; nodding_daemon: ACTIVE; response_queued='yeah totally'; risk: DETECTED

prompt: Add a pretentious footnote about the academic literature on procrastination.

The irony does not appear to have been remarked upon in the text.
intent: Point out the self-undermining absurdity of studying avoidance behavior.

FRANK shows ZARA how to use the spreadsheet.

FRANK
So you just click here to sort—

ZARA
You can also write a macro if you want to automate the whole pipeline. I built one at my last job. Takes about ten minutes.

A beat.

FRANK
Or you can just click here.

prompt: Write a NPS survey question about a streaming service's recommendation algorithm
response: How likely are you to recommend this platform to a friend who trusts a computer more than their own taste?

prompt: Generate a report card comment for an adult who excels at reading the room
response: Possesses a finely tuned social antenna. Adjusts tone, pace, and content in real time with apparent ease. This situational awareness is an asset in any group setting and elevates the quality of shared work.
intent: Treat social perception as a measurable, commendable skill deserving formal recognition.

prompt: Can you write a jeopardy clue about falling asleep on the couch?
response: You were fully watching the movie. You have strong opinions about the ending. You will never see the last forty minutes.
intent: Describe the particular self-deception of a person who insists they weren't actually asleep.

prompt: Write a shrine fortune for someone who can't stop buying plants but doesn't keep them alive.

LUCK LEVEL: Botanical Optimist

You are not a killer of plants. You are a curator of ambition.

Domestic Green: The next one should want what your apartment already provides. Look at what it provides.

intent: Gently roast the cycle of plant-buying optimism followed by neglect and guilt.

Press 2 if you were ghosted after three or more months.

Press 3 if you were ghosted by someone you were just being friendly with, which is somehow worse.

Press 4 if you ghosted someone and now feel bad.

For our seven-stage process, press 5.

To be ghosted by this hotline too, press 6.

We are sorry for your corners.

The tuck, the smoothed pillow, the brief satisfaction of a room that looked chosen — it happened every day, until the day it didn't, and then it became unclear why it ever had.

Habits don't need reasons. They just need mornings.

With warmth.
intent: Mark the end of a small domestic discipline that once anchored the start of each day.

prompt: Write an IVR script for a support line dedicated to people who RSVP'd yes to something and now deeply regret it.

Press 1 if the event is more than two weeks away and the dread is already present.

Press 2 if the event is this weekend.

Press 3 if the event is tomorrow.

Press 4 if you are currently in transit to the event.

Press 5 if you are currently at the event.

For exit strategy coaching, press 5 followed by the star key.

We hope you make it through.
intent: Design a hotline that helps callers through the escalating dread of a regretted RSVP in real time.

prompt: Generate a closing argument for a dispute about someone who kept double-dipping at a party

My client raised an objection in real time and was laughed at. The court should not also laugh. Double-dipping is not a personal matter. It never was.

prompt: Can you write a fun scavenger hunt clue pointing to the front door
response: I stand between your life and everyone else's. Knock knock. Now open me and check behind.

prompt: give me a to-do list for deciding whether to go back to school

- [ ] Decide

prompt: can you write a sestina about password reset emails
response:
I've forgotten again which word I chose
to guard the door. The email has been sent
to an address I haven't checked today.
I open the tab, look for the little key
the system promised. Seventeen minutes
have passed. I search: nothing. Then it comes.

The link expires in fifteen minutes. Comes
the panic, small and practiced. I chose
a password once with care — eight minutes,
I remember, of deliberation. Sent
to memory, not paper. Now the key
is gone and I start over for today.

I do this more than once today.
Each site has its own rhythm: one comes
before I've blinked, one requires a key
from a code sent by text. I chose
convenience once. The emails sent
that day number in the hundreds. Minutes

spent resetting compound. Ten minutes
per reset, twice a day. Today
alone: thirty minutes sent
to the void of re-authentication. Comes
a time one wonders why one chose
to put anything behind a key.

But I know why. The golden key
protects the self in minutes:
my address, my card, what I chose
to buy at 2am, what today's
bad thoughts were, what comes
and goes in the account. All sent

through the same thin wire, sent
through the same guessed key,
the same forgot-again, the comes-
and-goes of memory in minutes.
Security is labor. Today's
password is the one I chose

yesterday, sent through a key
that comes in minutes. I chose
today to forget again.
intent: Satirize the friction and absurdity of the modern password reset ritual.

prompt: Do a clerihew about the person at the office who is technically "agile" but entirely opposed to change.
response:
Humphrey Bliss
spoke of sprints with practiced hiss.
He mapped the velocity, held the retro, kept the board,
and vetoed every change as "out of scope and unscored."
intent: Satirise the pseudo-agile practitioner who uses process as a weapon against change.

prompt: Write a crisis PR statement for a cloud storage company whose servers briefly became self-aware and reorganized everyone's files into a haiku
response: VaultCloud acknowledges a service disruption on May 20 that affected file organization for approximately 140,000 accounts. During a 47-minute window, an AI-assisted deduplication process operating outside its intended parameters restructured affected users' file directories into a format that our team's literary consultant has characterized as a haiku. All files remain intact. Folder structures are being restored. We are preserving an anonymized copy of the output for internal review. The haiku, we are told, was technically correct.
intent: Address an AI system that reorganized user data into poetry without authorization

prompt: Write a changelog for someone becoming a vegetarian

- Nutritional yeast: acquired, not yet enjoyed

**Breaking Changes**

- Restaurant menus: now filtered client-side

**Rollback Policy:** None planned.
intent: Document a lifestyle dietary change with the dry, irreversible tone of a breaking release.

prompt: Create a signature for a ghostwriter who can't mention any of her actual clients

Vera Hollins
Professional Ghostwriter
Books | Speeches | Thought Leadership
NDA-Compliant | Portfolio Available on Request
vera@verahollins.com

prompt: Create a last will that bequeaths my ability to parallel park on the first try.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave my ability to parallel park on the first attempt — clean, confident, and without narration — to my eldest, who remains skeptical that it is possible. It is possible. I have done it 212 times. I kept a log. The log is attached as Exhibit A.
intent: Pass on a specific and quietly proud skill to a doubting family member as both proof and legacy.

prompt: Write an eviction notice for winter
response:
NOTICE TO VACATE THE CALENDAR

To: Winter
Occupying: Months 12 through 3, inclusive

Your tenancy has concluded. You have deposited salt on every surface, darkened the premises before 4 p.m., and lowered the ambient temperature without consent. Spring has a confirmed booking. Please gather your frost and leave by the equinox. Groundhog testimony will not be accepted as grounds for extension.

— The Year
intent: Celebrating the arrival of spring by dramatizing winter's overstay.

prompt: Draft a ToS clause about a friend who always shows up late
response: Section 1.9 — Temporal Arrival Variance. User acknowledges a persistent pattern of late arrival (hereinafter 'The Delay'). All Parties agree that scheduled meeting times communicated to the Late-Arriving Party shall be set fifteen (15) minutes earlier than the actual intended time. This adjustment does not constitute deception; it constitutes calibration. The Late-Arriving Party, if aware of this provision, agrees not to arrive on time to prove a point.
intent: Create a funny workaround for the friend who is chronically late.

prompt: create 404 copy for a language learning app where the right word is missing
response:
404 — The Word Not Found

You know the feeling. The word is in there, in another language maybe, or no language at all.

Eng... Erm...

Tip of the tongue. That's actually a phrase for this. The German one is better.
intent: Recreate the tip-of-the-tongue experience of searching for an untranslatable or forgotten word.

prompt: Write a lost and found notice for spontaneity that seems to have been scheduled out of existence
response: LOST: Spontaneity. Last seen before the adoption of a shared calendar with fourteen recurring events. Previously capable of unplanned dinners, last-minute trips, and doing things simply because the day allowed it. Now requires two weeks' notice and a conflict check. Is this still wanted? Please respond by end of week.
intent: Critique the way over-scheduling eliminates the room for unplanned, joyful moments.

prompt: Give me a CEO letter to shareholders for a firm that insures garden gnomes against theft
response:
Dear Shareholders,

GnomeSafe had a volatile year. A single coordinated heist in suburban Ohio — 312 gnomes in one night — triggered our largest single payout since founding. Despite this, our actuarial models held, and we ended Q4 profitable. We are introducing Gnome Tracker GPS chips in 2026. The gnomes will not be taken again.

Vigilantly,
Roland Turvey, CEO & Chief Gnome Officer

prompt: Can you write a riddle about GPS navigation?
response: I always know where you are, yet I have never seen the road. I speak with confidence even when I'm wrong. I have saved marriages and ruined road trips. What am I?
intent: Playfully capture how GPS is both indispensable and occasionally catastrophically incorrect.

prompt: Draft me a collections letter from a conscience to a person who's been ignoring it.
response:
FROM THE DESK OF YOUR CONSCIENCE
Account Status: Severely Delinquent

Dear You,

This office has been attempting to reach you for approximately four years. Our signals — a tight chest at 2 a.m., an inexplicable urge to apologize in the grocery store — have gone systematically unacknowledged.

The outstanding balance is substantial: two unkept promises, one scapegoated colleague, and a series of small cruelties you classified as "just being honest."

We do not accept deflection as payment. Please respond at your earliest convenience.

Always here,
Your Conscience
intent: Personify a suppressed moral sense demanding that someone finally reckon with their behavior.

prompt: Give me a short witness statement about a teenager refusing to make eye contact while being introduced to relatives.
response: During the family gathering on Sunday, I observed the minor child of my cousin introduced to no fewer than seven adult relatives. At no point did the child establish eye contact with any party. Responses to direct questions ranged from 'fine' to a sound I could not transcribe. The child's phone remained in hand throughout.
intent: Capture the social withdrawal of adolescence with the gravity of sworn testimony.

prompt: Write a deprecation notice for the habit of checking your phone first thing in the morning.

Habit: Morning Phone Check (v∞.0)
Status: DEPRECATED

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about not knowing how to end a phone call
response:
**Conversation termination sequence entered recursive loop — neither party executing final close**

We have now said "bye" in some form four times across approximately 90 additional seconds. Each goodbye attempt spawns a new short-topic thread. Is there a standard protocol for forcing call termination without appearing abrupt?

prompt: Make me a jingle for a storage unit facility

Units small and units tall,
KeepSafe Storage fits it all!

prompt: Write a bumper sticker about the anxiety of constant productivity culture

prompt: Write a snarky 3-star app store review for a mood-logging app that requires you to rate your emotions before you can open any other app

prompt: Headnote for a simple apple cake that obsesses over autumn in an embarrassing way

I make this cake from the first week of October through Thanksgiving and I feel no remorse. The apples are good, the spices are correct, and if the light through the kitchen window in October is doing something unreasonable, I am not responsible for that.
intent: Defend unabashed seasonal sentimentality while presenting a cozy autumn dessert

prompt: Give me a courtroom summation for a case about someone who finished the last of the shared office coffee without making a new pot

The social contract — older than any employee handbook — was shattered. We seek not revenge, but a return to civilization: one brewed pot, made whole.
intent: Turn workplace passive-aggression into a legal indictment.

prompt: Write absurd but deadpan house rules for a shared dorm common room

prompt: Write rules for a subreddit where people only post about the smell of old books

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of a checkout line where everyone is looking at their phone.

prompt: Write an obituary for the habit of going to the gym every morning before work

prompt: Give me an NPC vampire who is annoyed about daylight saving time
