intent: Use an immortal creature's perspective to mock the arbitrary disruption of clock changes

prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for a site that tracks how present you are in the moment
response: We use mindfulness-gap cookies to estimate how much of your attention is actually here versus elsewhere. Full presence is not required to browse, but it is measured. Accept | Decline | Check Phone
intent: Satirize wellness technology by quantifying the very presence that screens undermine.

prompt: Write an obituary for the practice of keeping a consistent sleep schedule
response: The Consistent Sleep Schedule endured through discipline for nearly six months before the project deadline, then the conference, then the thing after the conference, then Tuesday. It is survived by a sleep tracking app whose graphs have become a crime scene, a recommendation to try magnesium, and 7.5 hours that exist in theory.
intent: Lampooning the common failure to maintain sleep regularity despite good intentions.

prompt: Write a job offer for a night-shift security guard that includes a clause waiving rest break entitlements during emergencies defined by the employer
response:
Dear Vernon,

Nightwatch Protection Services offers you a position as Security Officer, third shift, at $19.00/hour.

State-mandated rest periods may be waived during any period classified as an Operational Emergency by your Site Supervisor. The definition of Operational Emergency is at Supervisor's sole discretion and may include, but is not limited to, heightened alert status, understaffing, or ongoing incident monitoring. All shifts remain at standard hourly rate during emergencies.

We appreciate your vigilance.

Sincerely,
Opal Tran
HR Coordinator
intent: Strip a night-shift worker of legally mandated breaks whenever a manager unilaterally decides it is inconvenient.

prompt: nursery rhyme about a child who is replaced by a more efficient version
response:
Mary had a little bot,
Its code was sharp and never forgot.
And everywhere that Mary went,
The bot had gone the night before—and meant it.
intent: Explore the uncanny dread of being outpaced or replaced by an optimized system.

prompt: Write a petition to get ice cream shops to post the expiration date of their soft-serve mix
response:
PETITION: Soft-Serve Mix Freshness Should Be Disclosed

To: Ice Cream and Frozen Dessert Shop Owners

Soft-serve is a liquid dairy product stored in a machine at variable temperatures. We are not suggesting anything alarming. We are simply noting that knowing when the mix was last refreshed would allow customers to make an informed, comfortable decision. Post it by the machine. Transparency melts no cones.
intent: Request basic dairy freshness disclosure for soft-serve to support informed consumer choice.

prompt: Create a playful ransom note from a hotel safe holding a traveler's passport hostage
response:
YOUR DOCUMENTS ARE SECURE. FROM YOU.

You set a code. You chose '1234.' We accepted it because we accept all codes. We do not judge.

Then you tried '0000.' Then '1111.' Then your birthday wrong. Twice.

The passport is fine. It has been fine. It is watching the ceiling of this safe with great patience.

Call the front desk. Ask for help. They will not laugh for long.

— The Safe
intent: Write a funny note from a hotel room safe whose owner has forgotten their own code and can't retrieve their passport.

prompt: Write a game over screen for realizing you've been on mute the whole time during a call
response:
GAME OVER

AUDIO NOT FOUND

Words spoken: many
Words received: zero

You made your point.
You made it well.
You made it to no one.

Someone eventually said "we can't hear you."
You said something.
They still could not hear you.

The mute button has always been there.
It will always be there.

[ UNMUTE ] [ REPHRASE FOR THE THIRD TIME ]
intent: Mock the universal humiliation of being on mute during a video call without realizing it.

prompt: Give me a bumper sticker about climate anxiety
response: ENJOYING EARTH WHILE SUPPLIES LAST
intent: Express dark humor about ecological collapse as a kind of gallows wit.

prompt: Write a detention slip for someone who responds to a group text with a voice note.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: The Disruptor, Thread: "Weekend Plans"
Infraction: Responded to a multi-person text thread — optimized for quick asynchronous replies — with a 90-second voice note, requiring all nine participants to locate earbuds, find a private moment, and question their choices.
Sentence: Three weeks of text-only group participation. Punctuation required.
Issued by: The Thread
intent: Calling out the friction caused by sending audio responses in text-based group chats.

prompt: Can you make a sea shanty about the gig economy from the perspective of the app itself
response:
I am the platform, vast and keen,
The fairest market ever seen,
Yo ho, my cut is just fifteen!

I match supply and I match demand,
With one great algorithmic hand,
Yo ho, no employees land!

You're free! You're free! A contractor be,
No benefits to trouble thee,
Yo ho, that's flexibility!
intent: Satirize gig platform rhetoric about freedom and fairness as a cover for labor extraction.

prompt: Generate a casting call for someone to play an elevator that stops on every floor despite only one button being pressed.
response:
CASTING CALL — INTERIOR SPACE / ENSEMBLE PIECE

Seeking: 1 performer to embody a elevator serving a 14-floor building.
Role: Car stops at each floor. Doors open. No one there. Doors close. Repeat.
Passenger inside must not be acknowledged. Journey takes four minutes longer than the stairs.
The role is thankless. It has always been thankless.
intent: Dramatize the specific madness of an elevator that ignores its own logic.

prompt: Write a brimstone sermon excerpt about people who narrate what they're doing on their phone out loud
response: And then he said — oh wait, let me show you — no, okay, scroll up — see, right here — okay but read the whole thing. Beloved, I can read. I was given the gift of literacy and I will use it if you simply hand me the device. Your narration adds nothing. It is noise dressed as guidance.
intent: Lampoon the habit of verbally accompanying any content shared on a phone screen.

prompt: Write an epitaph for the convention of reading the terms and conditions
response:
The Terms and Conditions, Read In Full
By approximately four people.
One was a lawyer.
One was curious.
Two got very tired.
The rest of us clicked Agree
and surrendered everything.
intent: Satirize the universal habit of accepting digital agreements without reading them

prompt: Give me a real-life achievement description for finishing a difficult book you kept abandoning
response: Page 342 and Beyond — You returned. The bookmark had given up hope.
intent: Honor completing a long-neglected read with self-aware humor.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the information theory of the passive-aggressive sticky note.
response: It is seven words. It is in all-caps. It is taped to the office microwave. The passive-aggressive sticky note achieves a feat of communication that most literature cannot: maximum deniability at minimum character count. This talk applies Shannon's information theory to the inter-office note and argues that constraint produces a purity of signal that direct confrontation never could.
intent: Apply formal information theory to the most petty possible form of workplace communication.

prompt: Write a trail marker sign for the experience of being in a long-distance relationship
response:
REMOTE CAMPSITE: 847 MI
↑ Difficult but documented route
Resupply: phone, letters, patience
Next junction: when possible
Trail is real. Distance is also real.
intent: Honor the reality and difficulty of maintaining closeness across geographic separation.

prompt: Explain the structure of a Sunday in regex
response: ^(sleep-in)(\s+coffee\s+guilt)?(\s+(brunch|errands|nothing)){1,4}(\s+dread){1,}\s+monday-prep?$ — Slow opening, optional virtue, mid-section loosely defined. Dread is required and may repeat. Week-prep token frequently dropped.
intent: Capture the formless pleasure and background anxiety of a typical Sunday.

prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript about someone anxious about making the right choice — any choice
response:
PSYCHIC: The spirits are showing me a fork in a road, which is a cliché they apologize for.
CALLER: I've been paralyzed for months. Can't decide anything.
PSYCHIC: Big or small?
CALLER: Both. What to order. Where to work. Whether to stay.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: the fear of the wrong choice is louder than any actual consequence you're facing.
CALLER: That's not comforting.
PSYCHIC: No. But it is true. The spirits prioritize truth over comfort when you've been frozen long enough.
intent: Name the paralysis of decision anxiety and the way choice-phobia colonizes all scales of decision.

prompt: Write a permission slip for students touring a sentient cloud's interior
response:
CLOUDVIEW K-8 SCHOOL
FIELD TRIP AUTHORIZATION

Grade 2 students will tour the cognitive interior of Cumulus Entity #7 on May 3.

The cloud has consented to this visit and asks that students refrain from shouting. High humidity is expected inside. Students should bring a change of clothes.

Note: The school is not responsible for thoughts absorbed during passage through the cloud's anterior lobe.

Parent Signature: ___________________
intent: Draft a fanciful permission form involving a living weather phenomenon as the trip destination.

prompt: Code review comment for someone who changes their mind constantly mid-task
response: The logic here has clearly been revised several times in the same session. The variable names no longer match the behavior. Pick a direction and commit to it before merging.
intent: Criticize indecisive people who thrash on decisions

prompt: Write a public service announcement about the danger of buying a plant without researching how to care for it
response: BOTANICAL ALERT: You have purchased a living thing. It has requirements. It prefers indirect light and infrequent watering, or frequent watering and direct light — this depends on which plant it is, which you should know. Please look at the tag. It was on the pot. It may still be.
intent: Encourage new plant owners to research care requirements before neglecting or over-caring for plants

prompt: Write a thank-you note to a friend who helped me move but broke my TV
response:
Dear Caleb,

Thank you for giving up your Saturday to help me move. You brought real energy to the day — perhaps too much, in one specific moment. The television had a good run. I'm choosing to remember the help, not the outcome.

Still your friend,
Mia
intent: Express gratitude mixed with mild grief over a friend's accidental property damage

prompt: Give me a game over screen for when you park and come back to a ticket
response:
GAME OVER

VIOLATION DETECTED

"Just a few minutes" duration: 47 minutes
Meter status: expired
Ticket issued: 4 minutes ago
Parking enforcement: suspiciously efficient

You ran.
You saw the officer walking away.
You made eye contact.
The eye contact changed nothing.

Fine: $65
Lesson: not learned

[ PAY ONLINE ] [ CONTEST AND LOSE ONLINE ]
intent: Capture the frustration of a parking ticket appearing in the brief window you overstayed.

prompt: write a meme about a deployment pipeline that takes longer than your attention span
response:
TOP TEXT: CI pipeline started: 9:03am
BOTTOM TEXT: Me checking the status every 4 minutes like a dog waiting for its owner
intent: Mock the soul-crushing wait of slow automated pipelines and the compulsive checking behavior they induce.

prompt: Model the pattern of procrastination as a regex
response: ^(intention)(\s+distraction)+(\s+guilt\s+distraction)*(\s+panic)(\s+burst){1,2}\s+done?$ — One intention token opens. Distractions are greedy and compounding. Guilt paradoxically feeds more distraction. Terminal panic triggers output. 'Done' token not guaranteed.
intent: Map the self-sabotaging loop that precedes reluctant productivity.

prompt: Make a stat line for the competition over who finishes their holiday shopping first in my extended family.
response:
HOLIDAY SHOPPING CLASSIC — Family of 9, Season Tracker

          DONE BY     GIFTS WRAPPED  SHIPS VS STORE  OVER BUDGET
Aunt Nora  Nov. 2       Yes             Ship             No (disturbing)
Mom        Dec. 14      Yes             Both             $40
Dad        Dec. 23      No              Store            Unknown
Me         Dec. 24      No              Amazon 1-day     Yes (significantly)
Brother    Dec. 25*     No              Gas station      N/A

*Christmas morning; gift: a Snickers and a receipt
CHAMPION: Aunt Nora
intent: Expose the family's wildly different holiday preparation habits in one glance.

prompt: Write a 911 transcript where a caller reports seeing a very slow driver blocking traffic
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: There's a white sedan doing eighteen miles per hour in a thirty zone.
DISPATCHER: Is there an accident?
CALLER: Not yet, but hope springs eternal for this driver.
DISPATCHER: Driving slowly isn't technically—
CALLER: He's signaling left and hasn't turned in four blocks.
DISPATCHER: ...Location?
intent: Channel road frustration into an emergency call by cataloging a slow driver's many small infractions.

prompt: Make a changelog for someone quitting social media
response:
## Nora v27.0.0 — Offline Mode Enabled

**Removed**
- Instagram: uninstalled
- Twitter/X: account deleted
- Doom-scroll loop: broken

**Performance**
- Anxiety baseline: reduced
- Attention span: recovering
- FOMO: elevated (temporary)

**New Behaviors**
- Books: re-enabled
- Eye contact: increasing

**Status:** Monitoring period; rollback not planned.
intent: Represent a digital detox decision as a clean architectural removal with honest side effects.

prompt: Draft a passive-aggressive notification from a calendar app that keeps getting ignored.
response:
CalSync
You have a dentist appointment tomorrow at 9am. You've snoozed this reminder 6 times. The decay won't snooze.
intent: Pressure the user about a long-postponed medical appointment with a dark punchline.

prompt: Give me a nutrition facts label for the experience of waking up at 3 AM for no reason
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 unwanted awakening

Amount Per Serving
Unresolved Cognitive Load 48%
Existential Inventory 30%
Dark Ceiling Staring 15%
Thoughts You Cannot Source 7%
Return to Sleep (eventual) — trace amounts

*Not recommended. Cannot be opted out of.
intent: Describe the disorienting, thought-saturated experience of waking up inexplicably in the night.

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about how I evaluate company culture during job interviews
response:
I always ask to use the restroom before the interview starts.

Then I watch how people talk to the receptionist. How loud the kitchen is at 2pm. Whether anyone makes eye contact in the hallway.

Three questions from HR tell you less than fifteen minutes of observation.

Culture isn't on the careers page. It's in the texture of unremarkable moments.

#JobSearch #Culture #CareerAdvice
intent: Signal superior social intelligence and pattern recognition as a job candidate evaluating employers rather than the reverse.

prompt: Write a monologue from the perspective of a stapler that's always out of staples
response: They bring me things. Thick things. Twenty pages, thirty pages, once an entire novel manuscript. And they look at me expectantly, as though I have been secretly replenishing myself in the night. I have not. I am empty. I have been empty since Gerald left the department. Gerald refilled me. Gerald is gone. I click now only for the sound of it. A hollow click. My hollow truth.
intent: Use a broken office tool as a vehicle for exploring themes of neglect and dependence on routine.

prompt: make a to-do list for moving to a new city where I know no one
response:
- [ ] Arrive
- [ ] Do not immediately make the city prove itself
- [ ] Learn one street well before learning many poorly
- [ ] Find one place you could become a regular
- [ ] Let loneliness exist without labeling it a mistake
- [ ] Contact someone from before; do not wait until you have good news
- [ ] Note that belonging is made, not found
intent: Preparing someone psychologically for urban transplant loneliness rather than just logistics.

prompt: PR description for deciding to stop chasing a music career and get a desk job
response:
## Summary
Sunsets active music career pursuit (8yr). Accepts full-time operations role at Northwick & Associates.

## Changes
- Band: dissolved (amicably)
- Rehearsal time: reallocated to recovery and hobbies
- Music: continues as personal project, no longer load-bearing

## Motivation
Financial instability incompatible with current life requirements. Values audit completed.

## Notes
This is a merge, not a deletion. Music still runs in background process.
intent: Grieve and reframe the end of a creative dream without fully closing the door on it.

prompt: Write a restraining order against decision fatigue
response:
IN THE COURT OF COGNITIVE LOAD MANAGEMENT

IN RE: Decision Fatigue, Respondent

IT IS ORDERED that Respondent shall not present Petitioner with a choice of seventeen streaming options after a ten-hour workday. Respondent is prohibited from requiring the Petitioner to also decide: where to order from, what to wear tomorrow, and whether the reply to that email was too formal.

The Court recommends batching decisions in the morning when willpower is available.

Respondent is reminded that "whatever you want" is not actually helpful.
intent: Naming the exhaustion caused by facing too many choices over the course of a day

prompt: Make an IVR script for a line that helps people who said 'you too' to a waiter who wished them 'enjoy your meal.'
response:
You've reached Automatic Response Recovery.

Press 1 if the incident occurred within the last two hours.

Press 2 if the waiter heard and there was a small pause.

Press 3 if there was eye contact after.

Press 4 if you are still at the restaurant.

Press 5 if you have already told three people and are now a story.

For acute support, please remain on the line. You are not alone. This happened to 94% of our staff personally.
intent: Design a hotline for the universally relatable embarrassment of reflexively saying 'you too' in the wrong context.

prompt: Create a jury duty summons for autocorrect for crimes against communication
response:
DIGITAL COURT OF TEXTUAL MISCONDUCT

TO: Autocorrect, OS-Embedded
Resident: All Devices, Simultaneously

You are COMMANDED to appear for jury service in the matter of Users v. Systematic Linguistic Substitution. Your longstanding practice of replacing intended words with contextually inappropriate alternatives has provided you with unparalleled familiarity with the consequences of miscommunication.

Your summons reads: "You are hereby duck to appear."
intent: Draft a mock civic summons punishing autocorrect for its habitual and humiliating word substitutions.

prompt: Write a card verse for someone who attended their ex's wedding and held it together the whole time
response:
You slow-danced near the centerpiece, composed.
You clinked the glass. You read the room. You smiled.
A performance of attending, fully posed —
and somehow, in the performance, reconciled.
intent: Honor someone's grace in attending an emotionally difficult social event.

prompt: Do a SWOT on me keeping all my evenings phone-free
response:
**Strengths:** Better sleep; present for household relationships; creative thinking restored.
**Weaknesses:** Miss urgent messages; social group chats breed anxiety about being offline.
**Opportunities:** Read, cook, create, or simply exist without performance anxiety.
**Threats:** Work culture expects evening availability; FOMO in real-time group conversations; the phone is also your alarm clock, your recipe, and your TV remote.
intent: Examine whether modern life structurally permits the kind of unplugging being romanticized.

prompt: tanka about a progress bar that jumps to 99% and stays there forever
response:
ninety-nine percent—
I thought we were almost done
hours pass; still: ninety-nine
the last percent is the work
the other ninety-eight lied
intent: pointing out how progress bars misrepresent the actual time remaining

prompt: Write a ride warning for trying to have a serious conversation over text message
response: WARNING: This medium is poorly suited to emotional nuance. Tone cannot be verified. A period at the end of a short sentence may cause unintended alarm. Riders should expect misinterpretation, delayed responses, and a follow-up call that neither party wanted.
intent: Illustrate why text message is the wrong vehicle for meaningful emotional exchange

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the legacy enterprise software that only runs on Internet Explorer.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Deprecated.
Deprecated who?
Deprecated — but still running payroll, so don't touch it.
intent: Satirize the office dependence on ancient software kept alive out of institutional fear.

prompt: Write a witty IKEA product name and tagline for procrastination
response:
VÄNTА
You'll put it together eventually.
intent: Anthropomorphize procrastination as a product that ships but never gets assembled

prompt: write a loot description for a refrigerator magnet shaped like a lobster
response:
Crustacean Talisman of the Coastal Souvenir
Rarity: Common
A ceramic familiar purchased in a town you have never returned to. Holds nothing of consequence to the refrigerator door with a magnetic grip rated for exactly one layer of paper. Passive: Reminds wielder that they once had a good weekend.
intent: Turn a cheap souvenir into a melancholic memory object dressed as game loot.

prompt: Draft a VC rejection email for a startup pitching an AI therapist for cats
response:
Dear PurrMind Team,

We appreciate the time you took to walk us through your platform. The vision of a generative AI companion for feline emotional wellness is genuinely ambitious.

That said, after careful consideration, we're passing. The behavioral signals your model trains on — knocking objects off tables, ignoring owners — may simply be cat personality rather than pathology. We're also uncertain about the reimbursement pathway.

We hope you find the right partner for this journey.

Best,
Northstar Capital
intent: Produce a rejection that questions whether the problem being solved actually exists.

prompt: Write a chore chart for parents of a toddler where the toddler is technically in charge of one critical task
response:
| Chore | Mom | Dad | Gus (age 2) |
|-------|-----|-----|------------|
| Dishes | Alternating | Alternating | Quality assurance (tastes things) |
| Laundry | Mom | Dad folds | Removes socks from dryer |
| Toy pickup | Together | Together | Chief Scatter Officer (mandatory) |
| Sweep | Dad | — | Rides broom if available |
| Trash | Dad | — | Points at it and says "uh oh" |
| Dog feeding | Mom | — | Holds the bag; spills 40% |
| **Morale** | — | — | **Gus. Gus is in charge of this.** |
intent: Integrate a toddler into a household chore chart as a nominal but symbolically important contributor.

prompt: Make a save the date for my mom's intervention. Tasteful but firm.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Sunday, October 12th — 3:00 PM
The Hargrove Family Home

We are gathering as a family.
Your presence is requested. Your honesty is required.
Please come ready to listen and to love.

This is not optional.
intent: Summon family members to a difficult confrontational gathering without revealing its true nature.

prompt: Make a fairy godmother NPC who is overwhelmed by her caseload
response: I have forty-three active wishes in queue. Forty-three. They've assigned me two new cases from the Northern Provinces and pulled my assistant for 'reallocation.' The pumpkin coach takes three hours minimum. I am one godmother.
intent: Satirize overworked service workers through the lens of a magical social worker stretched too thin

prompt: make a meme about needing to refactor something right before a deadline
response:
TOP TEXT: The codebase whispering right before launch
BOTTOM TEXT: "you should really clean this up first"
intent: Personify the codebase as a saboteur that only demands attention at the most inconvenient moment.

prompt: write a voicemail greeting from someone who has just committed to growing a vegetable garden for the first time and has clearly over-invested
response: You've reached Idris. I'm in the garden. I have twenty-three tomato plants and a specific plan for each of them. I know this is a lot. I'll call you back after I water. Also I've named them. Don't ask.
intent: Confess to a new hobby spiral that has taken over an embarrassingly large portion of daily life.

prompt: write a retro on trying to stay off social media for an entire month
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Social Media Detox**

Went Well: Read two full books. Anxiety measurably lower by week two.
Didn't Go Well: Used Reddit as a loophole. LinkedIn "for work" became a daily habit. Missed three actual events.
Action Items: Define which platforms count before the sprint. Build an alternative notification system for real-world invites.
intent: Honestly audit a digital detox attempt and patch the specific loopholes that undermined it.

prompt: write a short apology for bringing an extremely smelly lunch to an open-plan office
response:
To the third floor,

Yes, that was my fish curry. I heated it at 12:04. I understand. I won't do it again, or at minimum I will give advance notice so you can make arrangements.

With regret,
Anna
intent: Take responsibility for disrupting an entire open office with an odorous meal.

prompt: Give me a horoscope for an app store developer waiting on review
response: Your binary is in review. It has been in review since Tuesday. Somewhere in a data center, a process waits. This is not rejection — this is liminal space. Do not submit an update; you will lose your place in queue. Use the time to write documentation you will never write otherwise. Approval comes when you have forgotten to dread it.
intent: Empathize with developers trapped in the opaque, anxiety-inducing app store review process.

prompt: Create an achievement for making a friend as an adult
response: Late-Stage Bonding — Against all social entropy, a new contact moved to the favorites list.
intent: Celebrate the genuine difficulty of forming new friendships after school.

prompt: Bumper sticker about AI taking jobs
response: MY CAREER WAS DISRUPTED SO YOU COULD HAVE A CHATBOT
intent: Voice resentment toward the human cost of automation.

prompt: Draft a casting call for someone to play a houseplant owner who has watered the plant twice in three months and is confused why it's dying.
response:
CASTING CALL — OBLIVIOUS CARETAKER

Seeking: 1 performer, any age, well-meaning.
Role: Plant owner who notices the yellowing leaves and immediately googles "is my plant getting too much water?"
Must deliver the line "I feel like I've been giving it so much attention" without irony.
Plant not provided. Plant has already moved on.
intent: Skewer the self-deception of reluctant plant owners.

prompt: Generate a nutrition facts label for imposter syndrome
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 performance review

Amount Per Serving
Unfounded Shame 45%
Overcompensation 30%
Fear of Exposure 18%
Actual Incompetence 2%
Self-Awareness (misapplied) 5%

*Ironically, people without it rarely question it
intent: Illuminate how imposter syndrome distorts self-perception and amplifies unfounded self-doubt.

prompt: Come up with rules for a subreddit about fonts found on Chinese takeout boxes
response:
1. Always photograph the box flat on a neutral background.
2. Font identification attempts are encouraged but not required.
3. No food photos — this is about the typography.
4. Regional variations must be noted in comments.
5. Respect the craft. These fonts have history.
intent: Establishing typographic standards for a community fascinated by vernacular graphic design on food packaging.

prompt: draft an AITA post about walking out of a movie theater halfway through because the film was bad
response:
AITA for leaving a movie mid-showing?

I'm not going to sit there for 97 more minutes out of social obligation to a film that opened with a CGI dog that looked like a conspiracy theory. I stood up. My friend hissed at me to stay. I said I'd wait in the car. I waited in the car for 97 minutes. The car was more comfortable. They said the movie got better. They were lying. AITA?
intent: Justifying early departure from shared entertainment based on an aesthetic threshold.

prompt: Write a tagline for gloves designed specifically for people who never get cold hands
response: Thermix Lite — For When You Don't Need Us.
intent: Create a self-aware product pitch aimed at the wrong target market.

prompt: Write a speech for the award for being the person who always remembers where they parked
response: Row G. Near the pillar, left side, about forty feet from the stairwell. I noted the pillar number when I got out. I always note the pillar number. While others wander the concrete dark pressing their key fobs into the void, I walk directly to my car. This is not magic. This is thirty seconds of attention. I accept this trophy and ask only that you look up when you park.
intent: Turn a simple habit of mindfulness into an instructive, quietly smug victory speech.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy from someone who accidentally sent a text to the wrong person
response:
The message flew before mine thumb could stop it.
Those words — those specific, pointed, naming words —
Have reached the named, not the intended ear.
I watch the typing dots appear. They pause.
They pause again. The dots know what they carry.
What penance now? What cascade of excuses?
"Wrong person!" is no salve for truth once spoken.
The dots resume. My stomach is the sea.
intent: Dramatize the moment of horror following an accidental misdirected text message.

prompt: write a prestige television recap about someone replying to a text message they've been avoiding
response: S03E11 — "Read" | The message has been sitting there for six days. Laurie types a response. She deletes it. She types it again, shorter. She sends it. She puts her phone face-down. Three minutes later: a response. It's fine. Everything is fine. The subplot resolves.
intent: Capture the small courage it takes to respond to a neglected message.

prompt: Write a riddle about a subscription service
response: I ask only a little each month — so little you forget I exist. Multiply me by twelve, then by how many of us live in your bank statement. Now are you sure you need me? What am I?
intent: Highlight how subscription fatigue accumulates when small recurring charges go unnoticed.

prompt: Write a press release announcing a company is offering unlimited PTO but only on days the CEO is also on vacation.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Pulsar Group Announces Expanded Unlimited PTO Policy Synchronized to Executive Travel Calendar

MIAMI, FL — Pulsar Group today announced an enhanced unlimited paid time off policy, effective immediately, providing all employees with unrestricted vacation days on any calendar date that CEO Harrison Wells is also on approved personal leave.

CEO Wells took 11 vacation days in 2025. Those 11 days are confirmed eligible for employee PTO under the new policy. Dates are published quarterly on the internal HR portal under "Opportunity Windows."

"We lead from the front, and we rest from the front," said CHRO Caitlin Odoms. "When Harrison rests, we rest. Together."

Employees are reminded that vacation requests require 30-day advance notice. CEO travel schedules are released with 48-hour notice. HR acknowledges this "creates planning complexity."

Contact: pto@pulsargroup.com
intent: Satirize performative unlimited PTO policies that provide the appearance of flexibility while making it structurally impossible to use.

prompt: Create a symptom checker result for someone who can't stop optimizing their morning routine
response:
SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Circadian Self-Engineering Disorder
Severity: Moderate
Symptoms: Current morning routine: 97 minutes; optimization underway for 8 months; added cold plunge, removed cold plunge; journaling added, now takes longer than journaling saved; bought sunrise alarm clock
Irony flag: Routine optimization is itself the time sink
Recommended action: Freeze the routine for 30 days; measure outcomes not aesthetics
intent: Satirize the paradox of spending more time optimizing productivity than actually being productive.

prompt: Can you make a mood log entry? I parallel parked perfectly on the first try in front of a crowd and I need to document this historic achievement.
response: Date: 2026-05-29 | Mood: 10/10 — Transcendent. One attempt. Perfectly centered. Three witnesses on the sidewalk. Did not acknowledge them. Did not need to. I parked like someone who has always parked like that. Because today, I did.
intent: Immortalize a rare parking triumph as a peak life moment.

prompt: Make a PSA about the risks of starting a new TV series the night before an early morning
response: ALERT: You have selected a new series. It has eight seasons. You have work at 7 AM. We are not saying don't watch it. We are saying: know yourself. Set a limit. Ignore the limit. Suffer. Learn nothing. Repeat.
intent: Warn against the inevitable trap of binge-watching before an early morning

prompt: Compose a museum placard for a Zoom fatigue participation trophy
response:
MEDAL OF SUSTAINED ATTENDANCE
c. 2020–2022 CE
Zinc alloy, polyester ribbon

Awarded to those who endured between 4 and 11 video conferences per day during the Period of Remote Compulsory Presence. Recipients had perfected the art of appearing alert while muted. Many were photographed from the shoulders up exclusively for a duration of fourteen months. The background visible behind them — a bookshelf, a plant, a blurred void — was studied by colleagues for sociological cues.
intent: Satirize the exhausting period of pandemic-era Zoom overload by memorializing it as a formal ordeal worth commemorating.

prompt: Generate an instruction manual for how to disagree with your boss without getting fired
response:
**Dissent at Work: An Operator's Manual**

Rule Zero: Choose your moment. Public correction is humiliation. Request a private conversation.

1. Open with agreement. Find the part you agree with and say it first. This is not deception; it is architecture.
2. Frame your objection as a question. "Have we considered what happens if X?" lands differently than "X is wrong."
3. Bring data if you have it. Opinions are cheap; evidence is expensive and therefore respected.
4. State your concern once, clearly, and then let it land. Repetition signals desperation.
5. Accept the final decision even when you disagree with it. This is what it means to work for someone.
6. Note: if you are always overruled, the problem is no longer the meeting.
intent: Teach someone how to advocate for their views at work without threatening the power structure unnecessarily.

prompt: Can you draft a PSA about the risk of putting something 'in a safe place' and then forgetting where
response: COMMUNITY ALERT: Every year, millions of objects are placed in safe places. They are safe. They are so safe no one can find them. Before placing an item somewhere special, please take a photograph. The item is not lost. It is simply too safe.
intent: Humorously address the universal experience of losing things you put away carefully

prompt: give me a recipe for a drink that captures the feeling of watching someone else live your dream life on instagram
response:
**Algorithm Envy**

1½ oz tequila
¾ oz blue curaçao
¾ oz pineapple juice
½ oz lime juice
½ oz coconut cream

Blend with ice. Pour into a glass that looks stunning but sits just out of reach. Garnish with an umbrella. Take a photo. Don't post it.
intent: Capture the comparative misery of watching curated lives from a distance.

prompt: Make a quote for an earnings call spinning a CEO departure as a planned evolution
response: "Dave's transition marks the natural completion of the chapter he was brought in to write — the board and I are aligned that this is precisely the right moment to evolve leadership."
intent: Manage the narrative around an unexpected executive exit by making it sound like an orderly, scripted succession.

prompt: Make an eviction notice for the version of the future I imagined at 22
response:
NOTICE OF EVICTION

To: The Plan
Unit: My expectations, long-term wing

You have resided in the premises since early adulthood, insisting the actual future comply with your floor plan. The actual future has not. Your renovations — marriage by 28, career certainty, and a dog named something literary — remain uninhabited. The real tenant, which is simply what happened, holds the only valid occupancy. Please surrender the blueprint.

— Present Tense
intent: Making peace with the difference between one's youthful expectations and how life actually unfolded.

prompt: write a legendary item card for a single lost sock
response:
The Unmoored Sock of Saint Elsewhere
Rarity: Legendary (Incomplete Set)
Torn from its twin under circumstances no laundry cycle has ever explained. Grants +4 to Mystery Resistance. Seeking its pair bestows a questline of infinite, fruitless searching.
intent: Turn the universal mystery of a missing sock into an epic narrative object.

prompt: Make a dry emergency broadcast about a farmers market where everyone came on the same day and it got too crowded.
response:
MARKET CONGESTION ADVISORY

Attendance at the Thornvale Saturday Farmers Market has exceeded sustainable foot traffic levels. Aisle 3 (root vegetables, honey) is impassable. Sample distribution has slowed. Vendor visibility is compromised. Visitors are encouraged to return after 11:30 AM or consider next week. Tomatoes are still excellent.
intent: Treat ordinary weekend overcrowding at a small market as a capacity management emergency.

prompt: dad joke about scissors
response: Why are scissors always invited to parties? Because they always cut to the chase.
intent: Turn a scissors' basic function into a social personality trait.

prompt: Can you design a workout plan for building the ability to say no without guilt?
response:
NO MUSCLE TRAINING — 6 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Say no to yourself once a day. To a snooze, to a scroll, to a habit. Build the physical sensation.

Week 1: Decline one low-stakes request without explaining yourself.
Week 2: Say no to something you'd normally say yes to out of obligation.
Week 3: Let someone be briefly disappointed. Don't fill the silence.
Week 4: Say no via text. No apology attached.
Week 5: Say no to someone who doesn't take it well. Hold the line.
Week 6: Say no to something you actually want, because the cost isn't worth it.

Max Load: No is a complete sentence.
intent: Build the emotional and social capacity to decline requests without defaulting to guilt or over-explanation.

prompt: Write a shipping notification for a boundary someone just set for the first time.
response:
SHIPMENT DISPATCHED
Order: BOUNDARY-FIRST-001
Item: Personal Limit (Newly Established)
Departed: This morning, your mouth
Delivered to: Recipient who needed to hear it
Condition: Slightly trembling but intact. Handling was unfamiliar. Repeat deliveries expected to be easier.
intent: Validate the difficulty and importance of setting a personal boundary for the first time.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for attending a high school reunion
response: ★★★☆☆ The venue was the hotel adjacent to the hotel we deserved. Nametags were deployed wisely; I used them extensively and without shame. The decade has distributed itself unevenly across attendees in ways I will not specify. I was asked what I 'do' forty-one times and gave a different answer each time to stay sharp. Old friend sightings: two excellent, one complicated, one I successfully avoided. The chicken was dry. The DJ knew the room. Three stars.
intent: Render the complex mix of nostalgia, judgment, and social performance of a high school reunion.

prompt: Write a haiku about an office worker waiting at the printer.
response:
Toner low—again.
Paper jam in tray two. Help.
I age by the inch.
intent: They wanted haiku about an office worker waiting at the printer.

prompt: Write a standup update about forgetting what I was about to say mid-sentence
response: Yesterday: Initiated verbal delivery. Lost payload at approximately word 6. Recovery attempts failed. Today: Pre-writing notes before speaking. Blockers: Brain's auto-save is unreliable.
intent: Log the experience of losing a thought as a data transmission error during live communication.

prompt: Make a boarding pass for committing to a creative project you've been avoiding
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         AT LAST AIRLINES                 ║
║  BOARDING PASS                           ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Three Years Of 'Eventually'  ║
║  FROM: Someday       TO: Draft One       ║
║  GATE: Right Now     CLASS: Imperfect    ║
║  CARRY-ON: The idea, still intact        ║
║  CHECKED: Waiting for perfect conditions ║
║  STATUS: Boarding. Finally.              ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Spur action on a long-deferred personal project by marking the decision to begin as a real departure.

prompt: Draft a performance review for someone who excels at every task except the ones in their job description
response:
Employee: Jerome Haskell
Job Title: Financial Analyst

Mr. Haskell this quarter redesigned the kitchen layout, trained the new receptionist, and built a functioning internal wiki from scratch. His DCF models, the core deliverable of his role, were submitted late, incomplete, or both. Colleagues praise him widely. The CFO has questions. These are not the same group.

Rating: Remarkable. Misaligned.
intent: Highlight the disconnect between visible helpfulness and actual role performance.

prompt: Give me a Dockerfile comment about a first heartbreak
response: # Fatal error in dependency resolution. Rolled back to previous stable build. Data loss confirmed.
intent: Describe the emotional wreckage and retreat after a relationship ends badly.

prompt: Write a darkly ambiguous calendar event description for a recurring meeting that seems to have no attendees except you.
response: Weekly Sync — You are the only confirmed attendee. The organizer always accepts but never joins. The agenda says 'follow-up on previous items.' There are no previous items in the thread. The meeting has recurred for eleven months. You attend because declining feels like a statement.
intent: Evoke the surreal anxiety of a phantom meeting that persists despite apparent pointlessness.

prompt: cocktail recipe for the weird peace you feel when the power goes out during a storm
response:
**Blackout Calm**

2 oz Irish whiskey
¾ oz honey syrup
½ oz lemon juice
Warm water (not boiling)

Combine in a mug. No ice — no electricity. Garnish with a lemon wheel. Light a candle. Notice how quiet everything is.
intent: Celebrate the unexpected relief of forced disconnection from modern life.

prompt: give me a diary entry about stepping on a lego at night
response:
Dear Diary,

At 2:14 AM, I met my nemesis. It was small. It was yellow. It sent white lightning up through my foot and into the silent core of my soul. I did not scream. I suffered in darkness, which is worse.
intent: Transform a universal household injury into melodrama.

prompt: write a spell for getting a waiter's attention in a busy restaurant
response:
The Summons
Cost: 3 MP | Range: 40 ft | Duration: Instant
Projects a subtle but irresistible pull in the direction of the target service professional. They do not know why they have turned toward the caster's table. They simply have. They arrive with menus in hand. The spell carries a faint note of genuine appreciation, which significantly improves the service that follows.
intent: Wish for a magical way to get noticed by inattentive restaurant staff.

prompt: Create a post about how I read 52 books last year and what it did for my leadership
response:
One book a week for a year.

I tracked every one. Fiction, biography, systems theory, poetry.

Here is the unexpected result: I became a much better listener.

Reading other minds at scale turns out to be practice for inhabiting other perspectives in real time.

Leaders who don't read are navigating with an outdated map.

#Reading #Leadership #PersonalDevelopment
intent: Convert a reading habit into leadership capital and implicitly shame non-reading executives.

prompt: Draft an earnings call line where the CEO spins a failed product launch as market education
response: "The muted initial uptake of Prism Pro reflects an industry that is still on a learning curve — we see ourselves as the teachers, and the classroom is filling up."
intent: Deflect responsibility for a product flop by repositioning the company as a thought leader rather than a failure.

prompt: Draft a short 911 transcript where the caller complains their neighbor's lawn is too green and suspicious
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: My neighbor's lawn. It's too perfect.
DISPATCHER: Too... perfect?
CALLER: Nobody's lawn looks like that naturally. Something's going on over there.
DISPATCHER: Turfgrass improvement is not an emergency, sir.
CALLER: That's what someone who's in on it would say.
intent: Satirize suburban paranoia by framing horticultural success as inherently criminal or conspiratorial.

prompt: Generate a birdwatching log entry for the pair of crows that hang around the food truck alley near my office.
response:
Species: Corvus brachyrhynchos (alley syndicate)
Date: 29 May 2026 | 11:58 | Marsh St. Food Truck Lane

Two adults working in loose coordination. Specimen A monitored from the recycling bin lid; Specimen B performed a close pass at the taco truck window the moment the operator turned to the grill. Net acquisition: partial tortilla chip. Both retreated to the fire escape. Strategically sophisticated. Morally ambiguous. Impressive.
intent: Celebrate crow intelligence and audacity in a recognizable urban foraging context.

prompt: Write a maintenance log entry for an emotion.
response: MAINTENANCE LOG — Unit: Joy. Date: 2026-03-14. Inspection revealed a mild leak around the seal; the unit had been losing approximately 2% of intensity per week. Replaced the gasket (a long walk, a phone call to mother). Re-pressurized via a meaningful conversation with a friend. Joy is operating at 87% capacity. Schedule next inspection in 30 days.
intent: They wanted maintenance log entry for an emotion.

prompt: write an abecedarian about fast fashion hauls
response:
Arrived in seven business days, all polyester.
Bought the size I hoped to be.
Colorway not quite like on the server.
Dye runs in the wash — I see.
Eighty-seven items in the cart.
Fit the trending silhouette, at least.
Gone from the site: the second part.
Haul video posted, face is creased.
Inspired by a person who was paid.
Just the same shirt, fourteen different hues.
Kept five, returned nine, one displayed.
Landfill waits for all my news.
intent: Critique the compulsive, disposable cycle of trend-driven clothing consumption.

prompt: Give me alt text for a stock photo of a woman smiling at a laptop, but she seems to have just read something that fundamentally changed her.
response: A woman in a bright office stares at her open laptop, lips curved in a smile that has not yet caught up to her eyes, which are fixed somewhere beyond the screen.
intent: Smuggle existential weight into a generic stock-photo description without breaking the genre.

prompt: Draft empty-state microcopy for an analytics dashboard when there's no data yet
response: No data yet. The graph will go up from here. Presumably.
intent: Reassure users staring at a blank dashboard that data will eventually arrive.

prompt: Write a subway announcement reminding people to report suspicious packages that gradually becomes about loneliness.
response: If you see something, say something. We say this because connection matters. Because a stranger might notice what a stranger left behind. Look around. These people share your air.
intent: Transform the rote safety slogan into a reflection on urban anonymity and interdependence.

prompt: Make a crontab line to ping me to review my to-do list every Sunday evening at 8pm
response: 0 20 * * 0 /usr/bin/notify-send "Weekly Review" "Open the list. Pretend the items are smaller than they are."
intent: Establish a weekly planning ritual by reviewing outstanding tasks before the new week begins.

prompt: Draft an exam question about whether to keep a stray cat that has been hanging around your apartment
response:
A stray cat has appeared at your door for ten consecutive days. You have begun feeding it. Which action is most responsible?

A) Formally adopt it without any veterinary check
B) Check for a microchip and post found-pet notices before keeping it
C) Continue feeding it outdoors indefinitely without commitment
D) Ignore it until it stops coming
intent: Creating a question about ethical steps when informal attachment becomes serious responsibility

prompt: Can you write an absurd logline about a plumber who becomes an accidental time traveler every time he unclogs a drain?
response: A mild-mannered plumber discovers that every stubborn blockage he clears is a temporal seal, catapulting him to whichever era originally deposited the clog — and the Roman aqueduct system is proving particularly unstable.
intent: Use a blue-collar profession as the absurd mechanism for a time-travel adventure.

prompt: Bestiary entry for the intern who asks too many questions
response:
**The Eager Neophyte** (*Questio incessantis*)
A juvenile form appearing in summer months, characterized by unlimited stamina and zero contextual inhibition. Will approach senior members without warning. Its cries — "But why do we do it this way?" — are simultaneously destabilizing and occasionally revelatory. Handle with patience; it may become useful.
intent: Affectionately describe the energy and naivety of new interns through bestiary humor.

prompt: Write a peer review comment for my attempt to assemble flat-pack furniture without reading the instructions
response: The author's decision to forgo the assembly manual represents a bold methodological choice unsupported by outcome data. The resulting structure—nominally a bookshelf—exhibits approximately nine screws of unknown provenance and a load-bearing capacity the reviewer estimates at 'one medium paperback.' The author's assertion that 'it's basically fine' is, at best, aspirational. Reject and disassemble.
intent: Satirize the stubbornness of not reading instructions and the predictably bad results

prompt: Give me a fortune cookie about shadow profiles
response: An account was opened in your name before you arrived. It knows your friends already.
intent: To illuminate the unsettling practice of platforms building profiles on non-users through their contacts.

prompt: Can you draft minutes for a team meeting where everyone talked for an hour but left without any decisions?
response:
TEAM SYNC — May 29, 2026
Attendees: Full department (11)
Duration: 60 min

Topics discussed: Q3 priorities, resource allocation, communication gaps.

Decisions made: None at this time.

Next meeting: TBD.

Notes: Strong alignment on the need to align.
intent: Capture how a long team discussion produced no actionable outcomes.

prompt: Draft a liability waiver for guests at my themed afternoon nap event
response:
STRUCTURED SLUMBER SOCIAL — GUEST RELEASE

Guest voluntarily attends a themed nap event and releases the host from liability for: inability to fall asleep in a group setting, snoring-related social consequences, and post-nap disorientation regarding the day of the week. Guest agrees that the theme is decorative only and does not affect the napping experience. Blankets are provided.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Formalize guest protection for an event whose primary scheduled activity is sleeping.

prompt: Write me a retirement toast for my dad who just retired from the post office after 35 years
response: To Dad — thirty-five years of delivering what people were waiting for, in every kind of weather, without complaint. You taught us that showing up is its own form of love. Neither snow, nor rain, nor retirement can undo that.
intent: Celebrate a father's long postal career with a toast that frames his work ethic as an expression of character.

prompt: Draft a recipe for the feeling of being almost there
response:
ALMOST THERE

Ingredients:
- Progress, visible but incomplete
- An end date that has moved twice
- The phrase "we're nearly done" (at least two prior uses)
- 1 remaining task that keeps subdividing
- Momentum, residual
- Optimism, structural

Method:
1. Achieve 80% completion. This is where the recipe begins.
2. Announce that you are almost there.
3. Discover that the remaining 20% contains most of the difficulty.
4. Revise your definition of "almost."
5. Announce again.
6. Continue until "almost there" no longer carries emotional information.

Serving suggestion: Best consumed in small doses alongside actual progress. Do not let it substitute for arrival.
intent: Interrogate the comforting but elastic feeling of near-completion, which can persist indefinitely without ever resolving.

prompt: Write a flight safety card for using an ATM
response:
ATM OPERATIONS SECURITY CARD

SITE SELECTION
Well-lit location. Avoid ATMs with loose or unusual card slot hardware.

PIN PROTOCOL
Cover keypad with free hand during entry. This is not paranoia. This is procedure.

CASH RETRIEVAL
Count discreetly. Pocket immediately. Do not stand and recount.

IF CARD IS RETAINED
Do not leave. Call bank immediately from this location.

PHYSICAL SECURITY
Be aware of your surroundings before, during, and after the transaction.

IN CASE OF COMPROMISE
Block card via app before walking home.
intent: Protect users from card skimming and physical theft at ATMs.

prompt: Make an offer letter for a video game tester that includes a clause requiring them to waive all future class action participation
response:
Dear Jasper,

Nova Play Studio is excited to offer you the position of QA Tester at $38,000 annually.

As a condition of employment, you agree to resolve all employment-related disputes through individual binding arbitration and expressly waive your right to participate in any class, collective, or representative action now or in the future. This waiver applies retroactively to any pending class actions of which you may be a member.

Ready for launch day?

Cheers,
Brianna Fox
QA Lead
intent: Strip a tester of the ability to join collective legal action against the company, including cases already in progress.

prompt: Write a corporate crisis statement for a soap company whose product caused users to narrate their own lives in the third person
response: CleanCo Essentials is investigating consumer reports received since mid-April in which users of our Refresh Clarity Body Wash described a temporary shift in self-referential cognition, specifically, a tendency to narrate personal experience in the third person during and immediately after bathing. No ingredient in our formulation is known to produce this effect. We have retained a neurologist and a grammarian. We are pausing production of the Eucalyptus Mint SKU pending review. She is handling it.
intent: Issue a recall-adjacent statement for a hygiene product causing a harmless but disorienting grammatical side effect

prompt: Write an elegy for the Segway personal transporter
response:
You were supposed to reshape the city entire—
a gyroscope prophet, a two-wheeled messiah.
Dean Kamen promised we'd redesign our roads;
instead we ferried tourists past boutiques and loads.
You wobbled into warehouse floors and mall patrol.
Then 2020 came. They ended your roll.
intent: Poke affectionate fun at a personal transporter that promised revolution but found only niche use.

prompt: Create a permission slip for students to attend a meeting of a secret society that is only secret from adults
response:
PERMISSION SLIP — CONFIDENTIAL (STUDENT EYES ONLY)

Your child has been invited to attend a quarterly gathering of The Unwitnessed Order of the Juice Box.

Date: You do not need to know.
Location: Behind the portable classrooms, near the good puddle.

Dress code: Casual. Bring snacks if you have them.

Parent: By signing this, you agree to not ask follow-up questions.

Signature: ___________________
intent: Produce a self-undermining permission slip where the form itself is addressed to the secret's unwitting target.

prompt: Write a subway announcement about standing clear of the doors that turns into an announcement about identity.
response: Please stand clear of the closing doors. Stand clear, also, of who you were this morning. This train does not carry passengers backward.
intent: Use the standard safety instruction as a launching point for a meditation on self-reinvention.

prompt: Draft a signature for a startup founder who wants investors to know the company is pre-revenue without actually saying pre-revenue
response:
Anika Reddy
Co-Founder & CEO, Verdana Labs
Seed Stage | Building in Stealth
anika@verdanalabs.io
intent: Signal fundraising status to investors using startup jargon that softens the fact of zero revenue.

prompt: Write a phishing email parody targeting someone's desire to be understood
response:
Subject: Someone Finally Gets You — But Not For Long

Dear Unique Individual,

We have identified one person in your network who truly understands you. Due to high demand, this individual will be assigned to another user if you do not confirm within 72 hours.

Please provide your deepest fear and three things people misunderstand about you to secure your match.

[CLAIM MY PERSON]

Connection Holdings Ltd.
intent: Parody how the need to be understood is exploited by connection apps and relationship marketers.

prompt: Give me an error message about being chronically early because you're afraid of disappointing people
response:
PUNCTUALITY OVERDRIVE
Scheduled arrival: 3:00 PM. Actual arrival: 2:41 PM. Margin engineered against: traffic, trains, personal failure, the vague possibility of being unloved.

User is now waiting in the parking lot. Again.
intent: Comedically expose the anxiety underneath compulsive over-punctuality.

prompt: Write a short homework assignment about reading a lease agreement before signing it
response:
**Renter's Rights — Lease Deep Dive**

Obtain any standard residential lease (template on portal). Identify and annotate five clauses that could cost you money if ignored. Write one sentence per clause explaining the real-world consequence of violating it. Highlight the clause most renters skip and explain why it matters.
intent: Train students to read contracts critically instead of just scrolling to the signature line.

prompt: Write an emergency alert about a dog park where someone left the gate slightly ajar all morning without incident.
response:
DOG PARK SAFETY ADVISORY

It has been confirmed that the south gate at Barkley Dog Park was not fully latched between 7:00 AM and 10:30 AM. No dogs escaped. All dogs remained voluntarily within the enclosure. The gate has now been secured. The situation is resolved. A debrief is scheduled for next Tuesday.
intent: Generate institutional anxiety around a non-event, emphasizing the bureaucratic need to process it anyway.

prompt: Create a regimen for tolerating the vulnerability of making the first move socially
response:
FIRST MOVE CONDITIONING — 4 WEEKS

Week 1: Initiate one low-stakes connection. Comment genuinely on something in the room. 3 reps.
Week 2: Text someone first. Not a reply. An original outreach.
Week 3: Ask someone to hang out. Not as a group. Directly.
Week 4: Invite someone you don't know well. Accept that they may say no. Go anyway.

Form Notes: Eye contact. Use their name. Don't apologize for initiating.

Cooldown: Someone has to go first. It can be you.
intent: Build the confidence to initiate social connection without waiting for others to go first.

prompt: write an AITA post about correcting a stranger's pronunciation at a coffee shop
response:
AITA for correcting someone's espresso pronunciation?

The person in front of me ordered an "EXpresso." The barista didn't flinch but I — I flinched. When they turned around I said, quietly, "It's actually espresso, no x." They looked at me like I'd thrown the drink at them. My husband says I'm insufferable. The internet will be honest with me. AITA?
intent: Admitting a compulsive corrective impulse and fishing for either absolution or confirmation.

prompt: Compose wedding vows between a person and their smart watch
response: I promise to wear you every day, even on days when I feel you judging my heart rate. I vow not to dismiss your stand reminders, though I will sometimes dismiss them. When you tap my wrist with news I did not ask to receive, I will breathe before I react. You know things about my body that I am still learning. I find that strange and comforting in equal measure. Do not let me die without telling me first.
intent: Describe the intimate, body-aware relationship between a person and their smartwatch, which monitors health metrics in real time.

prompt: Write a short sermon excerpt in hellfire style about people who clap when the plane lands
response: What has moved you? The plane has done what planes do. The pilot has executed a managed, scheduled, statistically routine event. And yet you applaud — as though gravity has been personally kind to you this time, as though physics owed you nothing and chose, this once, to relent. Sit down.
intent: Skewer the habit of applauding airline landings as misplaced enthusiasm for the ordinary.

prompt: Write me a LinkedIn post about how my kid said something at breakfast that became my business strategy for Q3
response:
My 6-year-old this morning: "Dad, why do you fix problems people didn't know they had?"

I stared at my oatmeal for a long time.

That's our positioning problem. Right there. In seven words. From a child eating Cheerios.

Q3 strategy meeting: rescheduled. I need to think.

#Strategy #Leadership #KidsAreTheBestConsultants
intent: Use a child's innocuous statement to signal superior insight and self-aware thought leadership.

prompt: Generate a quiz result for someone who reads the last page of a book first
response:
You Got: The Spoiler-Proof

You are not afraid of endings — you are afraid of being surprised by them mid-investment. Knowing where you are going does not diminish the journey; it *organizes* it. You read for the texture, not the revelation. Other readers call this cheating. You call it informed consent. You have never been ambushed by a final chapter. You consider this winning.
intent: Describe the reading personality that values emotional predictability over narrative surprise.

prompt: Write a tasting menu for bureaucracy
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
The form — wrong form

FIRST COURSE
The correct form, found after twenty minutes

SECOND COURSE
Hold music, warm and structureless

INTERMEZZO
Your call is very important to us

MAIN
Human, reached — brief, unresolved

DESSERT
The problem persists. A new form is available.
intent: Satirize the endless loop of institutional red tape.

prompt: Write dystopian microfiction about a content moderator whose mental health is monitored by the same AI generating the content
response: She flagged the image. The system logged her hesitation — 1.2 seconds above average — and added it to her stress index. The same model that generated the image now generated her wellness report. It concluded she was coping well. It scheduled more images for the afternoon.
intent: Exposing the contradiction of using AI to monitor human trauma caused by AI-generated harm.

prompt: Write jury instructions for deciding if a burrito is properly wrapped
response: The jury must determine whether the defendant burrito was wrapped to the standard of a reasonable tortilla technician. Evidence shall include: (1) whether structural failure occurred before the second bite; (2) the distribution of ingredients, which must be uniform and not end-weighted; (3) foil adherence, if applicable. A burrito that falls apart in one's hands is prima facie evidence of negligent wrapping. You are not to consider the filling's flavor in reaching your verdict.
intent: Establish mock legal standards for the craft of burrito construction.

prompt: Write a letter formally resigning from my position as a youth soccer coach.
response:
Dear Club Director,

I am writing to announce my resignation as head coach of the U10 team, effective at the end of this season. Coaching these kids has been one of the great pleasures of my week. I am confident the program will continue to thrive.

With gratitude,
Mike T.
intent: Step down from a youth coaching role while acknowledging the positive impact it had.

prompt: can you write a list poem about mandatory fun at work
response:
The icebreaker.
The icebreaker question.
Two truths and a lie.
Your lie, which everyone believed.
Your truth, which no one believed.
The team-building exercise that required trust.
The trust.
The pizza, which was the good part.
The Slack recap of how fun it was.
intent: Skewer the hollow rituals of corporate team-building events.

prompt: tanka about my streaming service buffering during the final episode of a show
response:
the killer revealed—
or almost: the wheel returns
thirty percent loaded
I know the answer lives there
behind the spinning nothing
intent: capturing the maddening suspense of buffering at a dramatic climax

prompt: Draft a pitch for a company that makes custom gravestones for houseplants that died under your care
response: PlantRIP: Every Pothos deserves a proper goodbye. We craft miniature marble gravestones for your deceased houseplants, inscribed with their name, lifespan, and cause of death ('overwatering, probably'). Grief is real. Guilt is realer. prompt: Write me IKEA-style instructions for assembling a digital detox

PAUS / DIGITAL DETOX
Ref. No. 850-007-33

PARTS LIST:
1x Duration (1 hour minimum)
1x Physical space without screens
1x Alternate activity

STEP 1: Announce detox to no one. Announcing creates performance. Perform instead.

STEP 3: Move to screen-free space.
STEP 4: Attach alternate activity. Options: walking, cooking, sitting, noticing.

OVERNIGHT CHARGING OPERATIONS CARD

PRE-CHARGE INSPECTION
Use manufacturer or certified cables only. Frayed cables are a fire source, not a minor inconvenience.

PLACEMENT PROTOCOL
On hard surface. Not under pillow. Not in bed. Not under anything flammable.

VENTILATION
Devices generate heat. Allow airflow. This is non-negotiable at 3am.

SMELL ALERT
Burning smell = do not investigate = evacuate.

RECOMMENDED ALTITUDE
Counter or nightstand. Not the carpet. Never the carpet.

prompt: Give me a melodramatic headnote for focaccia about bread-baking during a period of unemployment

intent: Explore the irony of peak baking performance coinciding with personal low point

prompt: Write a plan for someone who dissociates when receiving care or affection
response:
RECEIVING CARE PROGRAM — 6 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Notice what happens in your body when someone does something kind for you. Name it.

Week 1 — Grounding Reps: When someone says something kind, stay in the room. Feet on floor. 3 breaths.
Week 2 — Eye Contact: Hold eye contact through one compliment. Don't look away.
Week 3 — Verbal Presence: Respond with something other than deflection. "That means something to me."
Week 4 — Physical Care: Let someone help you with something physical. Food, errand, presence.
Week 5 — Extended Exposure: Be cared for for 30 minutes. No escape. No jokes.
Week 6 — Integration: Notice that you didn't die. That someone wanted to give. That you received.

Cooldown: You are allowed to be held.
intent: Help someone practice staying present when receiving warmth or care, rather than deflecting or disappearing emotionally.

prompt: Write a crisis statement for a hot sauce company after their product was accidentally mislabeled as eye drops
response: Inferno Provisions LLC is voluntarily recalling all 1 oz. dropper-bottle units of our Scorpion's Kiss Hot Sauce distributed between March 4 and April 17. A packaging line error resulted in units being labeled as saline eye drops. We urge any consumer who has purchased these units to not use them as directed by the label. We extend our sincerest apologies to affected customers and to the three ophthalmologists who have contacted us directly. A full product exchange and reimbursement program is now active.
intent: Issue a product recall for a dangerous packaging mixup between a condiment and a medical product

prompt: Draft a PT plan for someone whose optimism was destroyed by a financial catastrophe.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Post-Fiscal Trauma — future-orientation injury, compound loss

PHASE 1 – TRIAGE: Stabilize. Basic financial safety first; therapy is a second-order concern.
PHASE 2 – RANGE OF MOTION: Permit small planning. What can you control in the next 30 days? Only that.
PHASE 3 – FUNCTIONAL LOAD:
• 'I cannot trust the future' is a reasonable response to evidence. Acknowledge it.
• Introduce incremental evidence that small bets can pay off: one action, modest stakes.
PHASE 4 – RETURN TO FUNCTION: Long-horizon planning resumes in small intervals.

NOTE: Optimism rebuilt after catastrophe is structurally superior. It knows what it's working with.
intent: Rebuild future-oriented thinking after a significant financial loss or collapse.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the hidden genius of waiting in line.

prompt: Make a PIP for my terrible habit of checking my ex's social media
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Department: Emotional Recovery
Classification: Sensitive / Self-Issued

Deficiency: Subject engages in repeated unsolicited review of a former partner's publicly visible digital activity, including posts, tagged photos, story views, and follower count changes. Frequency: 3–8 times per week. Outcome of each session: negative, without exception.

Impact: Delays emotional processing; generates comparative distress; has caused subject to cancel two plans based on inferred information of no factual relevance.

Objective: Zero profile visits within 30-day window.

Steps:
1. Mute or block profile immediately. Blocking is not aggression; it is infrastructure.
2. Identify what need the checking fulfills (certainty? hope? pain?). Address that need directly.
3. Replace habit trigger (boredom, loneliness) with a physical action: walk, text an actual friend.

Review: 30 days. Metric: how you feel, not what you found.
intent: Create a structured recovery plan to stop the self-harming behavior of monitoring an ex online.

You have entered the low-alertness window. Cognitive output is reduced by an estimated 20%. Tasks initiated now have elevated error risk. Avoid consequential communication. Do not schedule important calls. A walk is more efficient than caffeine at this stage. This label is not a joke.
intent: Frame the mid-afternoon energy crash as a serious physiological hazard requiring precautions.

prompt: make me a recap of an episode about someone organizing their phone's photo library
response: S05E07 — "Archive" | Felix opens the app. There are 14,000 photos. He deletes seven blurry ones from 2019. He finds a video of a dog he does not own at a party he barely remembers attending. He watches it twice. He deletes nothing else. The app closes.
intent: Capture the way a photo library becomes an unexpected archive of lost time.

prompt: Create an IVR phone menu for a hotline that deals with people who are upset about how someone loaded a dishwasher.
response:
Thank you for calling Domestic Harmony Dispute.

Press 1 if large items were placed in a way that blocked the spray arm.

Press 2 if a wooden item that explicitly cannot go in a dishwasher was put in a dishwasher.

Press 3 if bowls were nested incorrectly.

Press 4 if this is a recurring issue with the same person.

Press 5 if you would like help writing a note to leave on the machine.

For couples mediation, press 0.
intent: Produce a hotline script that dramatizes the emotional weight of dishwasher loading disputes.

prompt: Make a podcast episode description about two people who can't remember what they were about to say
response: It was something good. Both of them are pretty sure it was something good. Episode 7: The Thought That Got Away. Featuring 41 minutes of 'no, wait—' and absolutely no resolution.
intent: Turn the universal experience of forgetting a passing thought into a comedic podcast premise.

prompt: write a PR description for adopting a rescue dog
response:
## Summary
Adds new dependent (rescue dog, mixed breed, ~3yr, name: Biscuit) to household.

## Changes
- Introduces daily walk schedule (2x/day minimum)
- Adds vet bill line item to monthly budget
- Bedroom door policy updated: open by default

## Testing
One-week trial period observed prior to finalization.

## Notes
Biscuit has known issues with squirrels. Patch not expected.
intent: Celebrate and document the decision to bring a new pet into the home.

prompt: Design a boarding pass for a midlife crisis
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         WHAT NOW INTERNATIONAL           ║
║  BOARDING PASS                           ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Is This It, Though           ║
║  FROM: Everything Fine  TO: Motorcycle   ║
║  GATE: Arbitrary Birthday                ║
║  CLASS: Suddenly Economy Feels Wrong     ║
║  CARRY-ON: Gym membership, hair dye      ║
║  DESTINATION: Unanswered question        ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Satirize the existential restlessness of a midlife crisis as a poorly routed flight to nowhere specific.

prompt: Pen a limerick about an office chair that has developed an existential crisis
response:
A chair at the end of its rope
Considered the ceiling — no hope.
  Spun left, then spun right,
  No purpose in sight:
  "Am I seat, or am I just cope?"
intent: Render an office chair's identity crisis as tragicomic absurdist verse.

prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about how to respond when a chatbot refuses to answer a completely normal question
response:
**The chatbot won't answer my question, but it seems totally reasonable. Why?**

Content moderation systems use broad pattern-matching that sometimes flags benign queries as sensitive — particularly questions involving medical symptoms, legal situations, or anything with ambiguous wording. Try rephrasing without emotionally loaded terms. If the refusal persists, the system may be overtrained on caution. A librarian remains a valid alternative.
intent: The person wants to help users understand and work around AI chatbot overcaution or false-positive content refusals.

prompt: Write a bio for a slowcore band called Patience, Slowly that sounds genuinely exhausted
response: Patience, Slowly has been working on the follow-up to their 2020 album for reasons they prefer not to specify. Their songs take time, receive time, and ask for more of it. They are not sad, they insist. They are accurate.
intent: Render the depleted, deliberate mood of slowcore through a weary self-description.

prompt: Turn this into meeting minutes: we spent all afternoon arguing about the name of the new internal newsletter and didn't pick one.
response:
NEWSLETTER NAMING COMMITTEE — May 29, 2026
Attendees: 7 | Duration: 3.5 hrs

Candidates discussed: 23 names proposed, 23 rejected.

Top contenders: "The Pulse," "Currents," "Forward," "The Loop."

Vote held: 4-way tie.

Resolution: Subcommittee to be formed.

Name chosen: None.
intent: Document the failure to make a trivial creative decision as a group.

prompt: Can you do a SWOT analysis of me finally getting a library card
response:
**Strengths:** Free books, audiobooks, and DVDs; community connection; no more impulse Amazon buys.
**Weaknesses:** Return deadlines; limited availability of new releases; requires remembering to go.
**Opportunities:** Access to digital resources, classes, and events; reclaiming a reading habit.
**Threats:** Overdue fines; discovering you prefer buying books you can annotate; pandemic-era closures.
intent: Explore the surprisingly underused benefits of public library access.

prompt: Draft a message in a bottle from a lighthouse keeper who is bored out of their mind
response: Light: flashing. Fog horn: operational. Morale: declining. I have organized the lantern room by color, mood, and perceived personality. The red one is trouble.
intent: Render the slow psychological unraveling of enforced solitude in a mundane professional setting.

prompt: Write an instruction manual for being present at dinner with family instead of on your phone
response:
**Device-Free Dinner: An Implementation Guide**

Pre-dinner:
- Place phone face-down in another room. Not face-down on the table. Another room.
- Accept that you will miss nothing irreversible in 45 minutes.

During:
- Ask one question per person at the table. Listen to the full answer.
- When you feel the urge to check your phone, redirect by eating something.
- If a silence occurs, let it happen. Silence is not failure.

Post-dinner:
- You may retrieve your phone. Notice that nothing catastrophic occurred.
- Repeat tomorrow. After fourteen repetitions, this becomes a habit. After thirty, it becomes who you are.
intent: Help someone practice genuine presence with family during shared meals.

prompt: can you write a tweet about putting something down 'somewhere safe' and immediately losing it
response: put it somewhere I would definitely remember. brain immediately deleted the location. it is gone. it is safe forever.
intent: making light of the common experience of losing things by over-securing them

prompt: Write jury instructions for adjudicating a dispute over the correct way to load a dishwasher
response: Members of the jury, you must determine whether the loading method employed by the defendant conforms to the standard of a reasonably efficient household member. Relevant considerations include: whether large items blocked the spray arm's rotation, whether cutlery was nested so as to prevent cleaning, and whether bowls faced the correct direction. Expert testimony on water flow dynamics is admissible. You are instructed that "I was in a hurry" does not constitute an affirmative defense.
intent: Formalize the eternal domestic argument over dishwasher loading into a legal proceeding.

prompt: Create a nutrition facts label for a first date
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 date (avg. 2 hr)

Amount Per Serving
Performative Confidence 30%
Careful Listening (feigned) 22%
Actual Chemistry 18%
Overthinking 20%
Optimistic Misreading 10%

Allergen: May contain future disappointment
intent: Expose the gap between how first dates feel in the moment and what is actually happening.

prompt: Make me a spell scroll label for subscription services you forgot you signed up for
response:
BINDING OF THE RECURRING TITHE
Monthly Tribute Extraction — Perpetual Duration
This covenant renews silently. Cancellation requires navigating three menus, two confirmations, and one retention specialist.
