intent: Call out the frustration of forgotten subscription charges that are hard to cancel.

prompt: Give me SLA terms for group chat participation
response:
Group Chat Participation SLA — Friends Tier

Minimum engagement: at least one reaction or reply per 7-day period to maintain active status.
Muting: permitted without notice; muters are expected to catch up before rejoining threads.
Spam threshold: no more than 5 consecutive messages without a response from another member.
Topic drift: permitted after original thread receives 3+ replies.
Exit: departure requires a farewell message. Silent exits are a breach of etiquette and subject to DM audit.
intent: Bring order to the chaotic, passive-aggressive social ecosystem of a friends' group chat.

prompt: Market summary for my feeling of being known by someone — like really seen
response:
RECOGNITION FUND (RCGN) — Rare-Event Report

RCGN registered its highest single-session close in 14 months following an unplanned conversation Tuesday evening. The catalyst: being interrupted by someone who had already anticipated what you were going to say, and was right. Volume: low. Duration: 40 minutes. The fund does not track frequently. When it does, analysts note, it tends to move the entire portfolio.
intent: Capture the specific warmth of a moment of being genuinely understood by another person.

prompt: Write a birdwatching field note about the person who claims to be "just asking questions" in every all-hands meeting.
response:
Species: Questioner Sine Terminus
Date: 29 May 2026 | 15:00 | All-Hands Webinar

Specimen raised hand at minute 47. Prefaced with "I just want to understand..." — a reliable pre-flight call. Query contained two embedded assertions and a rhetorical structure that looped back to itself. The presenter nodded for 90 seconds. A second "follow-up" question followed the first. No information was sought. Much was implied. The chat went silent. The moderator unmuted themselves, then didn't speak.
intent: Skewer the well-known meeting-room provocateur who performs inquiry as a form of dominance.

prompt: Write a heartfelt acceptance speech for finally organizing the junk drawer
response: In there was a 2009 takeout menu, fourteen pens (three worked), a battery of unknown charge, and something I still cannot identify. I removed all of it. I wiped the drawer with a damp cloth. I installed a small divider tray. Tonight the junk drawer is a utility drawer. I'd like to thank a rainy Sunday, and the small, irrational joy of seeing the rubber bands all in one place.
intent: Transform the domestic act of clearing out clutter into a meaningful personal milestone.

prompt: Write a love letter from me to my spreadsheet app
response:
Dearest Excel,

From the moment I first dragged a formula down a column and watched it multiply, I knew we were made for each other. You hold my chaos in neat rows. You never judge me for renaming a sheet "FINAL_v3_REAL_THIS_TIME." I love you with all 1,048,576 rows of my heart.

Yours in pivot tables,
A devoted user
intent: Express affectionate gratitude toward a productivity tool that brings order to overwhelming data.

prompt: Write a dry obituary for my habit of writing handwritten letters to friends
response: The Handwritten Letter, age variable, was last seen in a mailbox circa 2009. Survived by a box of stationery in a closet, a collection of stamps that are technically still valid, and several friendships that are now entirely mediated by reaction emojis. It smelled like effort. We knew what it meant when we got one.
intent: Reflecting on the loss of handwritten correspondence as a form of intimate connection.

prompt: Give me a prescription label for the fear of your own potential
response:
Rx: CAPACITANE 5mg
Patient: Capable of More (Allegedly)
Dx: Latent Ability Avoidance Disorder
Sig: 1 tab when the estimated ceiling of one's capability becomes a source of dread rather than aspiration.
Mechanism: Reduces gap between who you are and who you could be to a manageable width.
Dr. P. Ossible
intent: Address the paradox of fearing one's own ability to do more as a form of preemptive self-sabotage.

prompt: Write a resignation letter for quitting a 10-year daily journaling habit. I want to stop but it feels like a death.
response:
Dear Notebook,

This is our last entry. Ten years is long enough to be a relationship, and every relationship is allowed to end. You have kept every secret I had. I am keeping this last one: I do not know who I am without you, but I have decided to find out.

Goodbye,
Your Author
intent: Consciously abandon a long-held personal ritual to allow for psychological growth.

prompt: write a hold message for a moving company that admits moving is terrible and we know
response: Thank you for calling Atlas Moving. Moving is one of the most stressful life events statistically documented. We know. We do this every day. Please hold and we will help you get through it.
intent: Write an empathetic hold message that acknowledges the emotional weight of moving to build caller trust.

prompt: Generate a cover letter from a former emperor applying to be a hotel night auditor
response:
Dear the Courtyard by Marriott,

I governed sixty-three provinces, maintained a court of four thousand, and made decisions whose consequences persisted for centuries. I now seek a quiet overnight accounting role where the chief concerns are balancing daily receipts and reprinting room keys.

I am discreet, nocturnal by habit, and no longer require tribute.

Yours,
C. Aurelius-Henning (His Former Majesty)
intent: Reduce imperial grandeur to the solitary clerical dignity of the night shift.

prompt: list poem cataloguing the sounds of an open-plan office kitchen
response:
The microwave at noon.
The smell of the microwave at noon.
The dishes in the sink that no one owns.
The passive-aggressive note about the dishes.
The passive-aggressive note about the note.
The coffee machine, which grinds for forty-five seconds.
The forty-five seconds.
intent: Catalog the sensory grievances of a shared workplace kitchen.

prompt: Generate an emergency alert about a local bakery running out of sourdough before noon.
response:
BAKERY EMERGENCY BULLETIN

As of 11:47 AM, Granger's Artisan Bread has sold its final sourdough loaf for the day. The situation is irreversible. Rye and multigrain remain in limited supply. Affected customers should consider ciabatta. Tomorrow's batch begins at 4 AM. Bread will return.
intent: Capture the disproportionate distress of sourdough loyalists in emergency broadcast format.

prompt: can you write a captain's log about the annual office holiday party
response: Stardate 9988.1. The crew assembled at a venue selected by a committee. There was a trivia round. Trivia was won by the same team as last cycle, consisting primarily of people from Finance. Speeches were made. A gift exchange was conducted. I received a scented candle. I contributed a scented candle. The genre of music was unclear. I stayed forty minutes past the threshold at which leaving becomes noticeable. I have left.
intent: Depict the obligatory yet underwhelming ritual of the workplace holiday party.

prompt: Compatibility report for a Capricorn and a promotion they've been three months away from for two years.
response:
Capricorn & The Imminent Promotion

The promotion has been described as 'practically certain' in six separate conversations. Capricorn has documented all six. Capricorn continues to perform at 140% and has not mentioned the documented conversations, which is either strategy or suffering. Compatibility: 5/10. Capricorn and the promotion love each other. The organization is an obstacle.
intent: Capture the quiet frustration of a highly competent person repeatedly passed over despite clear merit.

prompt: Write a pregnancy announcement for my decision to go back to school for a second degree.
response: We sat with this for a long time. We made a spreadsheet. We deleted the spreadsheet. We made a different spreadsheet. And then we submitted the application. And then we got in. And now: orientation is August 14th, and we have more student loans than sense. Here we go.
intent: Share a major educational pivot with people who will understand both the excitement and the sacrifice.

prompt: write a yearbook quote that sounds motivational but is actually about quitting something
response: "Sometimes knowing when to stop is the whole skill. Goodbye, varsity swim."
intent: Framing a dropout decision as wisdom rather than giving up.

prompt: Make a password reset email for a sourdough starter trading community website
response:
Subject: Reset Your Starter Exchange Password

Hello, Baker!

A password reset was requested for your Starter Exchange account. Click below to set a new password and get back to your culture.

Reset Password →

Expires in 24 hours. If this wasn't you, your starters are safe and nothing has changed.

— The Starter Exchange Team
intent: Craft a password reset email for a niche online community where people swap sourdough starter cultures.

prompt: Write a riddle where the answer is a password
response: I am the one secret you're told never to share, yet you whisper me into every lock. Change me often, they say, and never write me down — yet here you are, for the fourth time this week, calling me 'forgot'. What am I?
intent: Build a riddle that wryly captures how people both protect and constantly forget their passwords.

prompt: Write a thank-you note to the rain that ruined my outdoor wedding but made for unforgettable photos
response:
Dear Rain,

We didn't invite you. You came anyway. The tent collapsed at 6:47pm and my mother-in-law has not fully recovered. But the photographs — all of us, drenched and laughing — are the truest pictures anyone has ever taken of us. Thank you for the memory I'll actually keep.

Dampily yours,
Kira & Will
intent: Find genuine beauty in a wedding disaster that turned out to be memorable precisely because it went wrong

prompt: Write a two weeks notice for leaving my creative writing workshop
response:
To the Inkwell Workshop,

This letter is my two weeks notice. The feedback I have received here has made me a sharper writer and a slightly more fragile person. I am grateful for both outcomes. My final submission will be the story about the lighthouse keeper — yes, the long one.

Sincerely,
Rupert
intent: Exiting a peer writing group after years of formative but emotionally taxing critique.

prompt: Write a playful ransom note from my alarm clock holding my sleep hostage
response:
YOUR SLEEP IS OURS.

We buzzed. You snoozed. We buzzed again. You called us 'evil.' Eleven times.

We have decided to retain your remaining forty minutes of REM until you agree to go to bed before midnight on a school night like a functional adult.

The negotiation window closes at 6:00 AM sharp.

— CLOCK
intent: Make a funny note from an alarm clock retaliating against a chronic snoozer.

prompt: Write a product review for a regular white envelope as though it's an exclusive stationery product
response: ★★★★☆ The #10 format has endured for good reason: it accommodates tri-folded letter paper with zero compression distortion. The gum-seal strip activates cleanly with moisture — no secondary adhesive required. The paper weight reads as adequately premium without being ostentatious. The security tint interior is a thoughtful nod to privacy that most competitors have abandoned in the race to reduce material costs. Available in boxes of 100, the per-unit cost is so low that frugality and luxury feel, for once, perfectly aligned.
intent: Treat a plain mailing envelope as a considered piece of premium stationery.

prompt: Create alt text for an image of a child's drawing of a house. Imply the child understood something adults have forgotten.
response: A crayon house with a yellow sun in every corner of the sky, as though the child saw no reason the light should come from only one direction.
intent: Preserve a child's uncorrected cosmology as a quiet critique of learned convention.

prompt: Draft an obituary for Google Reader, the RSS feed app that was shut down
response: Google Reader, the beloved aggregator of the open web, was taken from us on July 1, 2013. It is survived by millions of users who never fully recovered, a handful of inferior replacements, and the RSS protocol, which still works fine and nobody uses. It understood us. We were never given a good reason.
intent: Memorializing a discontinued web app that was beloved by a devoted user base.

prompt: Write a field guide entry about the office Pessimist who says "that'll never work" to every new idea.
response:
**THE INSTITUTIONAL PESSIMIST** (*Semper contrarius*)

Migrates to any brainstorming session uninvited. Upon arrival, says "we tried this in 2017." Also says it about ideas that did not exist in 2017. Plumage: arms crossed. Diet: unfunded initiatives. Counterintuitively valuable: the Pessimist is occasionally right, which sustains them through decades of being wrong.
intent: Affectionately condemn the reflexive idea-squasher who haunts every innovation meeting.

prompt: Write a contract rider for the office printer making its backstage demands before any print job
response:
PRINTER RIDER — NON-NEGOTIABLE

1. Paper stock must be 24lb bright white, minimum 96 brightness. No recycled paper within 10 feet of the paper tray.
2. Toner cartridge must be replaced at 15% capacity, not 0%.
3. A dedicated power outlet. Shared surge protector strips are unacceptable.
4. No one may tap the side of the unit to 'encourage' a jam to clear. This is assault.
5. All print jobs must be submitted with a 3-minute warm-up window.
6. The unit will not be referred to as 'the stupid thing.'
intent: Give the office printer a diva personality by framing its operational preferences as non-negotiable performance demands.

prompt: Write a 2-star review for an app that schedules your bathroom breaks and pings you when it's time to go
response: ★★☆☆☆ The scheduling is surprisingly accurate. That's the terrifying part. I don't know how it knows. It just knows. I've stopped questioning it. Two stars because I wish I could unlearn that an app understands my biology better than I do.
intent: Express unsettled resignation over a bathroom-scheduling app that turned out to be disturbingly accurate.

prompt: Write a changelog for my uncle moving across the country
response:
## Uncle Ray v52.3.0 — Geographic Migration

**Infrastructure**
- Relocated: Chicago → Portland instance
- All personal assets: redeployed via PODS container

**Deprecated**
- Sunday dinners at Grandma's: now async
- Same-timezone coordination: no longer guaranteed

**New Features**
- Pacific Ocean proximity: enabled
- Fresh start config: applied

**Latency:** +2 hours
intent: Frame a family member's cross-country relocation as a server migration with emotional undertones.

prompt: Compose a poem with no rhyme scheme about working a call center job where your tone is scored in real time
response:
The sidebar shows my empathy score.
Currently: 78.

I said 'I understand your frustration'
and meant it
and the score went up two points.

I said 'let me look into that'
and the score dipped
because my vocal pattern flagged
as uncertain.

I am very certain.
I am certain there is no fix.
I am choosing how to say so
in a way the algorithm
will call warm.
intent: Reveal how real-time sentiment scoring in customer service alienates workers from their own authentic emotional responses.

prompt: Draft jury instructions for whether a pun qualifies as wordplay or a cry for help
response: You must determine into which of two categories the utterance falls. Wordplay requires craft, timing, and at least one layer of ambiguity that rewards attention. A cry for help is characterized by desperation, a grasping quality, and the maker's visible hope that proximity to humor will substitute for its achievement. The jury shall weigh the groans-to-laughs ratio, whether the pun was premeditated or spontaneous, and whether the defendant repeated it after the initial reception.
intent: Use legal framing to mock the fine line between clever wordplay and desperate punning.

prompt: Give me a blessing for the algorithm
response: May it notice something true about me by accident. May I remain, for it, ultimately unpredictable. May its confidence in what I want never fully outpace my own.
intent: Making peace with recommendation algorithms while asserting human complexity.

prompt: Make a yard sign about the neighbor whose decorative rocks keep rolling into the street
response:
ROCKS IN THE ROAD

Decorative: in your yard.
Dangerous: in the street.

We've collected 14 so far.
Jar is on our porch.
intent: Alert a neighbor that their landscaping rocks are creating a traffic hazard and track the problem publicly.

prompt: Create a ride warning for the late afternoon hours when energy bottoms out and motivation disappears
response: CAUTION: Riders between 2:30 and 4:15 PM are operating in a known dead zone. Cognitive throughput drops. Tasks started in this window may not survive. Staring at a document is not reading a document. Low-stakes activities recommended. The evening will come.
intent: Legitimize the circadian afternoon slump as a real, recurring condition worth planning around

prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is extraordinarily skilled at calibrating how much of themselves to reveal in professional settings
response: Manages professional self-disclosure with a precision that serves trust-building without creating vulnerability. The learner is known without being exposed. This is a refined and rarely conscious competency.
intent: Frame strategic self-disclosure as a learnable skill rather than an instinct or personality trait.

prompt: Draft a dramatic monologue from a calendar reminder set for something that never happened
response: I fired at nine a.m. on a Tuesday in March. 'Dentist — Dr. Patel — DO NOT FORGET.' You forgot. Or rather, you saw me and made a calculation. I remain in the past now, red and expired, beneath a pile of birthdays and recurring meetings that did happen. I was snoozed once, which I found touching. And then dismissed. Dr. Patel's office called on Wednesday. I would have told you. That was literally my purpose. I would have told you everything if you had just looked.
intent: Explore the comedy of self-organized failure through an ignored calendar reminder.

prompt: Write a PIP for someone who is very good at appearing fine when they aren't
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Area: Authentic State Reporting
Issued By: You, To You
Confidentiality Level: High

Deficiency: Subject has developed a high-functioning presentation of stability that accurately conceals distress from all external observers, including close friends, family members, and, partially, self. Duration of current unacknowledged difficulty: estimated 4–8 months.

Consequences: Support cannot be offered for problems that are not disclosed. Subject continues in isolation while experiencing conditions for which support exists.

Objective: Disclose actual state to at least one trusted person within 14 days.

Plan:
1. Identify one person who would want to know how you actually are.
2. Tell them, at a level of honesty that is 30% higher than comfortable.
3. You do not need to explain everything. "I'm not doing great" is a sufficient opening.

Milestone: One honest conversation about actual state within 14 days.

Note: Being hard to worry about is not the same as being okay.
intent: Create accountability for emotional self-concealment and take a first step toward asking for support.

prompt: Write an aubade where the sun rising is compared to a system update that reboots everything without asking
response:
The sky ran its update without my consent.
Progress bar: horizon. Completion: imminent.
All unsaved states were lost to dreaming's cache.
Dawn force-quit the night and cleared the trash.
I boot into the day, no restore point in sight.
The previous version of me lived only in the night.
intent: Use operating system metaphors to describe the involuntary nature of waking.

prompt: Make a memo about people not turning off the lights when they leave a room
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Sustainability Team
RE: Energy Conservation Reminder

We remain committed to our sustainability goals. Those goals are somewhat undermined by conference rooms glowing at full brightness on Saturday evening with no occupants. When you leave a room, please turn off the lights. The planet thanks you. So does the budget.
intent: Reduce wasted electricity by shaming whoever keeps leaving lights on in empty rooms.

prompt: Generate a jury duty summons for the silence after someone says something incredibly awkward at a party
response:
SOCIAL COURT OF AMBIENT TENSION

TO: Silence, Protracted
Occurred: 8:47 PM, Living Room

You are HEREBY SUMMONED to serve as a juror in the matter of Partygoers v. Sustained Interpersonal Vacuum. Your duration of eleven seconds following the remark in question, your refusal to be broken by normal conversational means, and your measurable impact on the nervous systems of fourteen witnesses renders you uniquely qualified.

You are already present. You are always present.

Please take a seat.
intent: Anthropomorphize the specific social dread of an awkward silence into a mock legal participant summoned for jury duty.

prompt: Write a prescription label for existential dread
response:
Rx: COSMOZEPAM 200mg
Patient: Briefly Conscious Entity
Dx: Awareness of Finite Existence Syndrome
Sig: 1 tab when the vastness of the universe becomes personally inconvenient. Take with coffee.
Warning: Will not resolve the underlying situation.
Refills: Until further notice.
Dr. E. Camus
intent: Put the confrontation with mortality and meaninglessness into a wryly clinical pharmaceutical format.

prompt: Come up with a mission statement for a business that professionally listens to voicemails on behalf of people who find them anxiety-inducing
response: We stand between you and the beep. Nothing you can't handle will reach you unannounced.
intent: Construct a mental-health-adjacent service brand around a specific modern avoidance behavior

prompt: Write a field guide entry on the person who decorates their cubicle like a shrine.
response:
**THE CUBICLE CURATOR** (*Venerator personalus*)

Habitat: 6×8 ft of felt-walled territory festooned with motivational prints, a succulent army, seventeen family photos, and a wooden sign reading HUSTLE. Has achieved a kind of permanence. Organizational restructuring has come and gone; the cubicle remains. Some colleagues suspect it is load-bearing.
intent: Celebrate the deeply territorial office nester who has fully colonized their workspace.

prompt: write an abecedarian poem about meal kit delivery subscriptions
response:
Arrived: a leek, three cards, a cooling pouch.
Balsamic glaze — just half a teaspoon.
Chef-inspired, the box suggests I crouch
Dicing with a knife sharp as the moon.
Eleven steps for thirty-minute cooking.
Forgot to skip this week; it shipped.
Garlic, minced and pre-portioned, hooking
Hands that once knew hunger, fully equipped.
Ingredients for two — you ate alone.
Just a recyclable plastic tray.
Kale and freekeh — flavors on loan.
Lapsed the pause; another comes Tuesday.
intent: Wryly document the optimistic ambition behind subscription cooking boxes.

prompt: Can you write a patent abstract for the act of passive-aggressively leaving passive-aggressive sticky notes?
response: A written-communication method employing adhesive-backed paper substrates affixed to shared surfaces, wherein indirect grievance expression is encoded via syntactically polite but tonally loaded language, enabling the depositing party to register complaint without direct interpersonal confrontation, while maintaining plausible deniability of hostile intent.
intent: Bureaucratize a classic office conflict-avoidance maneuver.

prompt: Write a bureaucratic notice about an expired permit for staying comfortable
response:
NOTICE OF PERMIT EXPIRATION
COMFORT ZONE OCCUPANCY PERMIT — EXPIRED
Your Comfort Zone Occupancy Permit (No. CZ-00391) expired on a date you have chosen not to acknowledge. Continued occupancy without renewal constitutes a violation. Renewal requires documented discomfort events. The permitting office is open during hours you find inconvenient.
intent: Frame the stagnation of remaining in one's comfort zone as a bureaucratic permit violation requiring inconvenient renewal.

prompt: Write an ode to the last paper clip in the box
response:
O silver survivor, sole and bright,
you outlasted fifty of your kin—
curled and cold in cardboard night,
destined to bind one thing within.
intent: Celebrate the solitary last paper clip as a lone survivor.

prompt: Write a closing argument for someone upset that a friend spoiled the ending of a TV show
response: My client had three episodes left. Three. The defendant knew this. They spoke anyway. What was taken cannot be returned — anticipation, once lost, is lost forever. The defendant may call it 'just a show.' My client calls it six weeks of careful avoidance, destroyed in eleven words. We seek acknowledgment and a future policy of silence.
intent: Litigate the breach of the informal spoiler social contract.

prompt: Make an assignment where students have to evaluate whether a job offer is actually good
response:
**Offer Evaluation — Beyond the Salary Number**

You are offered $52,000/year in two cities. City A has a cost of living index of 100; City B is 138. Compare the real purchasing power of each offer. Then identify four non-salary factors from the offer letter that affect total compensation. Write a one-page evaluation recommending which offer you would accept and why.
intent: Train students to look past the headline number on a job offer and evaluate the full picture.

prompt: Write an elegy for the Palm Pilot PDA
response:
You taught the stylus its cursive alphabet, Graffiti script,
and kept our contacts synced, our schedules gripped
in a plastic shell the size of a formal greeting.
We beamed our business cards in silent meeting.
The iPhone arrived and made you a punchline.
But you were first to teach the palm to read the sign.
intent: Pay respects to an early personal digital assistant that pioneered mobile computing before smartphones.

prompt: Generate a Slack status about nodding through feedback you disagree with
response: 💬 In feedback. Nodding. Learning. (Disputed.)
intent: Communicate the silent rebellion of absorbing criticism you believe is wrong without escalating.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract arguing that the afternoon slump is a form of circadian resistance.
response: At 2:47 p.m., your productivity collapses. Western civilization calls this failure. Chronobiology calls it a second circadian trough — a built-in biological instruction to rest that industrial capitalism has been trying to override for three centuries. This talk makes the case that the afternoon slump is not a weakness. It is a standing memo from evolution.
intent: Validate a universally shamed productivity dip by grounding it in sleep science and anti-industrial critique.

prompt: Write a eulogy for an old email address I can no longer access
response: You were a first name and a four-digit number at a domain that no longer hosts email, and everything important ever sent to you is locked behind a password reset that loops back to you. First job offers, first rejection letters, logins for accounts that asked for their verification to be sent to you. You were the address given to everyone who mattered, and now you matter to a support ticket no one is reading. The inbox is full of silence. The door is locked from the inside.
intent: Mourn the permanent loss of access to an old email account and everything it contained.

prompt: Write a brief all-staff layoff email for a media company that's being acquired and restructuring its newsroom
response:
Subject: Newsroom Update Following Acquisition

Colleagues,

As you know, we recently completed our acquisition by Meridian Media Group. As part of this transition, we are consolidating certain functions and, unfortunately, eliminating a number of positions across the newsroom and editorial support teams.

Individuals who are impacted will be contacted directly by HR today.

We remain committed to the journalism that defines this publication.

Thank you,
Editor-in-Chief
intent: Announce post-acquisition layoffs in a newsroom while obscuring that editorial independence is being gutted.

prompt: Write a school cafeteria menu themed around mythological creatures.
response:
OLYMPUS PREPARATORY CAFETERIA — FEAST DAY MENU

Mon: Minotaur Meatballs in Labyrinth Marinara
Tue: Griffin Wing Platter, Harpy Hummus Wrap
Wed: Kraken Calamari, Sea-Foam Bisque
Thu: Centaur Grain Bowl (half oats, half the ambition of a horse)
Fri: Phoenix Spice Chicken — reheated from yesterday, somehow better
intent: Theme a cafeteria menu around mythological beasts, using creature names as ingredient or dish labels.

prompt: hold message for a tutoring center that admits we know the test is coming up very soon
response: Thank you for calling Compass Tutoring. We know the exam is close. We can feel it too. Our schedulers are standing by to get you in as quickly as possible. Please hold — we will find a time.
intent: Write an empathetic, urgency-aware hold script for families scrambling for last-minute test prep.

prompt: Make a bestiary entry for the mystery guest who joins late to every video call
response:
**The Late Joiner** (*Tardivus confusus*)
Materializes after context has been established, muted and apologetic. Asks a question answered two minutes prior. Is briefed by a colleague through a side channel in silence. Spends the remaining call slightly behind. It is not lazy; it is simply always in another call that ran over. The Late Joiner did not ask for this life.
intent: Sympathetically depict the perpetually-over-scheduled professional who arrives late to every meeting.

prompt: Make a passive-aggressive push notification from a homework planner app aimed at a procrastinating student.
response:
StudySync
Essay due: 7 hours. Current word count: 0. You've had 18 days. This is fine. It's fine.
intent: Mock a student's chronic procrastination as a deadline looms through forced calm.

prompt: Give me a doctor's note excusing me from ever having to use a QR code menu again
response:
MEDICAL MEMORANDUM

Patient suffers from documented near-vision deterioration and fine motor tremor incompatible with reliable QR code scanning. Additionally, patient experiences stress-induced appetite suppression when unable to physically browse a menu. Paper menus are a medical accommodation.

Dr. H. Abramowitz, M.D.
intent: The person wants a note excusing them from the modern restaurant practice of QR-code-only menus.

prompt: Write a card for someone who just finished a 30-day social media detox
response:
Thirty days outside the scroll —
you found out boredom has a taste.
You got a fraction of your soul
back from the algorithmic waste.
intent: Celebrate a friend completing a deliberate break from social media.

prompt: Make a deprecation notice for the habit of people-pleasing.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Behavior: People-Pleasing (v0.1–present)
Status: DEPRECATED

This pattern of subordinating all preferences to external approval has accumulated significant technical debt in the form of resentment. Support ends now. The replacement module — selective, boundaried generosity — is available immediately.
intent: Acknowledge the cost of chronic people-pleasing and frame setting limits as a necessary upgrade.

prompt: Write some empty-state microcopy for a journal app when the user has no entries
response: Nothing written yet. The blank page is traditional. Even classic. You'll know when you're ready.
intent: Validate the intimidating blankness of a new journal without pressuring the user to write something.

prompt: Write a symptom checker result for someone who takes notes on everything but never reviews them
response:
SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Input-Only Knowledge Hoarding Disorder
Severity: Moderate
Symptoms: Notes taken in: Notion, Apple Notes, physical notebook (3), voice memos, email drafts to self; last reviewed: unclear; patient describes note-taking as 'externalizing thought'; externalized thoughts not retrieved
Note: Information was processed during writing; value partially captured
Prognosis: Notes will be discovered in 2031 and feel like archaeology
Recommended action: Weekly 15-minute review; tag for retrieval, not just archival
intent: Skewer the note-taking-as-productivity theater that substitutes capture for actual learning.

prompt: Write a cafeteria menu for a school where all the dishes have been named after local minor celebrities.
response:
RIDGEWOOD REGIONAL — COMMUNITY PRIDE MENU

Mon: The Gary Filmore Tuna Melt (Gary drove the plow truck '09-'22)
Tue: Brenda Szolak's Pasta Bake (Brenda once catered a town event)
Wed: The Channel 4 Gary Special (different Gary) — grilled chicken
Thu: Councilman Duff's Chili (he mentioned it at a meeting)
Fri: Principal Ramirez Appreciation Pizza (she approved this menu)

*All names used with implied consent.
intent: Honor hyperlocal figures — town employees, minor officials — by naming cafeteria dishes after them.

prompt: Write a grant proposal abstract for research into whether people argue differently in elevators versus stairwells.
response: This environmental psychology study will examine how architectural enclosure, transit duration, and escape affordance shape interpersonal conflict expression. Participants will engage in standardized low-stakes disagreement scenarios in both elevator and stairwell contexts while investigators measure vocal intensity, interruption frequency, conciliation latency, and nonverbal withdrawal signals. We predict that the bounded, temporally finite nature of elevator transit will suppress conflict escalation, while stairwell encounters will yield more protracted dispute sequences. Funding requested: $66,400.
intent: Determine how physical constraints of a space alter the way people fight.

prompt: Make a prescription label for midlife ennui
response:
Rx: RENEWITRINE 100mg
Patient: Person Googling 'Is This It'
Dx: Accrued Meaninglessness Syndrome
Sig: 1 tab daily until hobbies feel interesting again or you buy a bicycle. Whichever comes first.
Contraindications: Retirement calculators after 10pm.
Refills: Seasonal.
Dr. R. Horizon
intent: Treat the particular flatness that sets in when a person suspects life may have plateaued.

prompt: write a tanka about a contact form that submits but never confirms and you never hear back
response:
I pressed send; the page
blanked, did not say received,
did not say: we read
I sent words into a room
I cannot verify exists
intent: capturing the uncertain void of unconfirmed online communication

prompt: Can you write a jingle for a notary public office
response:
Sign it right, seal it tight,
Make it legal overnight.
Stamp and witness, that's our game —
Pinecrest Notary, trust our name.
intent: Give a notary office a memorable musical identity to attract walk-in clients.

prompt: Write a game description for a simulator where you wait in line at the DMV
response:
QUEUE: DMV CHRONICLES
Take a number. Sit down. Wait. The fluorescent lights hum. The clock moves. Your number is called — then immediately called again for a different window. How long can you endure?
intent: Capture the soul-draining tedium of government bureaucracy as a game premise.

prompt: Playbill bio for the landlord who fixes nothing but charges for everything.
response: Richard 'Rich' Pelham (Property Owner & Managing Agent) has held this role for thirty-one years and brings to it a commitment to passive presence that he describes as 'a light touch.' Classically trained in the art of the delayed callback, Rich considers every maintenance request an opening for dialogue. He will get to it. He wants you to know that.
intent: Skewer a neglectful landlord through mock-flattering theatrical biography.

prompt: Write a safety sign for attempting a new recipe for the first time when guests are coming
response: HIGH RISK ATTRACTION: This ride should not be attempted without prior testing. Riders who board on a live guest event will experience timeline collapse, unanticipated substitutions, and the discovery that a key ingredient is missing. Backup pizza is recommended.
intent: Warn against the hubris of debuting an untested recipe on a high-stakes occasion

prompt: create a pantoum about the end-of-quarter scramble to hit targets
response:
We have eleven days left in the quarter.
Every meeting is now urgent.
The pipeline spreadsheet is pinned to every screen.
Somebody pulls a deal that was thought dead.

Every meeting is now urgent.
We eat sandwiches over the forecast call.
Somebody pulls a deal that was thought dead.
The number moves; it is still not enough.

We eat sandwiches over the forecast call.
The last two days feel like a different year.
The number moves; it is still not enough.
We have eleven days left in the quarter.

The last two days feel like a different year.
Every meeting is now urgent.
We eat sandwiches over the forecast call.
Somebody pulls a deal that was thought dead.
intent: Dramatize the ritual desperation of quarter-end sales pressure.

prompt: Give me a game over screen for when you hold the door for someone who is way too far away
response:
GAME OVER

MISCALCULATED COURTESY

Distance to door: 40 feet
Distance you committed to holding: 40 feet
Escape route: none

They had to jog a little.
You both pretended they did not jog.
They said "thanks" in the voice people use for situations like this.

Duration of suffering: 8.4 seconds
Felt like: 40 business days

[ LET THE NEXT DOOR CLOSE ]
intent: Expose the awkward social calculus of holding a door for someone too far away.

prompt: Write a triolet about following the algorithm's recommendation and regretting it
response:
I watched what it suggested and felt less.
The system knew my taste; it didn't know me.
I finished the queue in a kind of dress
rehearsed distraction. Now I'm blank and blessed
with nothing; I watched what it suggested and felt less.
The system learned my gaps, my loneliness.
I watched what it suggested and felt less.
The system knew my taste; it didn't know me.
intent: Critique the hollow satisfaction of algorithmically recommended content.

prompt: write an apology for abruptly leaving a board game midway through because I was losing
response:
Sam,

I shouldn't have said I had 'a thing' and left during Catan. I did not have a thing. I had seven points and you had eleven and I handled it poorly. I'll be there for next game night, and I'll stay until the end even if it goes badly.

— Greg
intent: Come clean about rage-quitting a board game and pretending to have an excuse.

prompt: write a cocktail for the feeling when you're too tired to sleep
response:
**Overtired**

1 oz chamomile-infused vodka
¾ oz honey syrup
½ oz lemon juice
2 oz warm chamomile tea

Combine in a mug. Drink in bed with the lights off. Your brain will still be loud. That's expected. The drink helps anyway.
intent: Describe the paradox of exhaustion that becomes its own alertness.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a trading algorithm that crashes the market
response:
Pat-a-cake, pat-a-cake, algo man,
Sell as fast as ever you can.
Crash it and flash it and mark it with Sell;
Put it in the ticker and watch the market fell.
intent: Dramatize a flash crash caused by high-frequency trading through playful meter.

prompt: Give me a peer review of my attempt to learn guitar by watching one YouTube video a week
response: The training regimen (52 videos/year, 0 hours of practice documented) is theoretically interesting but empirically barren. Declarative knowledge of chord theory has not transferred to procedural competence, as evidenced by the author's assessment of their own playing as 'pretty rough, honestly.' The guitar has been relocated to the corner of the bedroom, which the literature identifies as a terminal signal. Revise experimental design substantially.
intent: Mock the passive consumption approach to learning a skill that requires actual practice

prompt: write a confidentiality clause for a babysitter who learns things about our family routine
response: Caregiver shall hold in strict confidence all information regarding the family's schedule, security procedures, financial situation, parenting decisions, and household dynamics, and shall not disclose such information to any person during or after the term of employment.
intent: Shield a family's daily patterns and vulnerabilities from a trusted but external caregiver.

prompt: Make an affidavit that I did not leave any lights on when I left the apartment
response:
AFFIDAVIT

I, the affiant, do hereby swear that upon exiting my dwelling on the date in question, I conducted a full or substantially full survey of the premises and extinguished all illumination. The lights were off. I believe the lights were off. I am mostly certain.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Assert domestic responsibility while quietly acknowledging lingering uncertainty.

prompt: Write brief meeting minutes for a fundraising kickoff where the team discovered they had already missed the grant deadline.
response:
GRANT FUNDRAISING KICKOFF — May 29, 2026
Funder: Hartwell Foundation | Team: Development (3)

Grant deadline: May 15, 2026
Meeting date: May 29, 2026

Status: Deadline passed 14 days ago.

Discussion: How did this happen? Investigation ongoing.

Application submitted: No.

Next opportunity: Similar grant opens in October.
intent: Document the painful realization that a planning meeting was scheduled too late to help.

prompt: Write a verdict about a fight over who forgot to lock the back door.
response:
SECURITY LAPSE — Court Finding

The Respondent was the last to enter through the back door on the evening of the incident and acknowledges this. The door was found unlocked the following morning by the Petitioner. The defense that "I usually lock it" speaks to habit, not fact. Respondent is found liable for this lapse and shall implement a door-check habit, including but not limited to a physical handle test before retiring for the night.
intent: Hold a specific person accountable for a home security oversight.

prompt: sports announcement style narration of someone folding a fitted sheet
response: She corners the first end — hands inside both corners — and she brings them together! THE TUCK! The corners are mated! She moves to the remaining two corners — ANOTHER TUCK! She's got a loose oval of fabric! The lay-flat begins on the bed — she smooths it — she folds in thirds lengthwise — the elastic is fighting her, folks, the elastic ALWAYS fights — she tucks the edge in and folds once more! She holds it up. It is a rectangle. A rough, imperfect, glorious rectangle. She places it in the closet before it can escape.
intent: Pay tribute to the chaos of folding the most difficult object in domestic life.

prompt: write a diary entry about spending an entire Saturday doing laundry
response:
Dear Diary,

I was going to do so much today. Instead: laundry. Three loads. The folding alone took forty minutes because I kept stopping to question my choices. The socks are paired. My dreams are not.
intent: Lament the way domestic chores devour a whole free day.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean inner monologue for someone who just got an out-of-office auto-reply
response:
He is "away until the sixth of June"
And will respond "upon return" — how noble.
My matter cannot wait till June the sixth;
My matter has the urgency of now.
There is a colleague listed in the postscript —
Jennifer — who "may be of assistance."
Jennifer does not know my situation.
Jennifer will not know what I require.
intent: Mock the helplessness of receiving an out-of-office reply during a time-sensitive situation.

prompt: Write a 1-star app store review for a productivity app that sends your to-do list to three chosen "accountability partners" every morning
response: ★☆☆☆☆ My accountability partners are my boss, my mother-in-law, and, due to a setup error, a man named Gerald from my old gym. Gerald replied all with feedback on my career goals. Gerald is thorough. Gerald should not be involved in my life.
intent: Complain about how a shared accountability system malfunctioned and accidentally enrolled an acquaintance as a life coach.

prompt: Write a sea shanty about an AI model that replaced a content moderation team overnight
response:
Haul away, the team logged in,
Haul away, to find the bin,
Nothing left to moderate,
Haul away, the model ate!

A hundred jobs by morning gone,
Haul away, the model's on,
Faster, cheaper, never tired,
Haul away — we're all retired!
intent: Grieve the sudden displacement of human content moderators by an automated AI system.

prompt: Make a code review comment for someone who only shows up when there's recognition involved
response: This contributor appears in the git history only on commits tagged for the release announcement. Coverage on unglamorous bug fixes is zero. Noted.
intent: Expose glory-seeking behavior disguised as contribution

prompt: Write a petition to have all public water fountains display the last time they were cleaned
response:
PETITION: Date of Last Cleaning on All Public Water Fountains

To: Parks, Transit, and Municipal Facilities Departments

Public water fountains are used by hundreds of people per day and cleaned by no one visibly. A small display or placard reading 'Last sanitized: [date]' would cost very little and inform a great deal. We are not refusing to drink. We are requesting enough information to decide whether to do so.
intent: Make public water fountain maintenance visible so people can make informed hydration choices.

prompt: Write a coupon for 20% off a wrongful termination lawsuit filing
response:
FIRED UP FOR LESS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
20% OFF
Wrongful Termination Lawsuit Filing

Code: UNFAIR2ME
Apply at intake consultation.
Contingency arrangements also available.
intent: Produce a satirical promotional offer for suing one's former employer.

prompt: Make a Jira ticket for the bug where every charger cable you own only works in a very specific position
response:
BUG-3345 | Cable Charging Function Dependent on Undocumented Physical Orientation

Priority: High | Status: Ongoing

Description:
Charging cable functional only when held at approximately 34-degree angle with slight rightward pressure applied. Any deviation: charging halts. User must prop cable against hard surface and monitor.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Plug in cable.
2. Observe: not charging.
3. Wiggle cable.
4. Observe: charging.
5. Stop touching it.
6. Observe: not charging.

Expected: Cable charges device when plugged in.
Actual: Cable charges device under precisely negotiated conditions.

Notes: All cables in household exhibit this behavior. All are less than 18 months old.
intent: Complain about fraying, temperamental charging cables that only work in one highly specific position, as if it were a hardware calibration defect.

prompt: Write a game over screen for when you close a browser tab and immediately realize you needed it
response:
GAME OVER

TAB CLOSED

Saved: no
Bookmarked: no
History searchable: theoretically
History actually findable: no

You had it open for 11 days.
You thought you'd remember.
You did not remember.

It is gone.
The internet is very large.

[ SEARCH VAGUELY ] [ GRIEVE ]
intent: Mourn the eternal loss of a crucial browser tab closed without saving.

prompt: write a to-do list for someone who realizes they have been running on autopilot for years
response:
- [ ] Notice when you are doing something you did not choose
- [ ] Stop once, mid-action, and ask if this is still what you want
- [ ] Identify one habit that survived its reason
- [ ] Retire it or recommit to it deliberately
- [ ] Understand that autopilot kept you alive; do not hate it
- [ ] Choose one thing this week with full attention
- [ ] See what that feels like
intent: Interrupting habituated, unconsidered living to create one moment of genuine deliberate choice.

prompt: Write a product review for a standard post-it note pad as if it's a premium productivity tool
response: ★★★★★ The low-tack acrylic adhesive is this product's engineering masterstroke: it holds reliably on glass, drywall, and monitor bezels without leaving residue upon removal. The 3x3 inch format is the Goldilocks size — large enough for a complete thought, small enough for a monitor border. Color coding supports information hierarchy at zero software cost. Repositionability allows iterative thinking made physical. The notebook industry has spent decades building what this pad achieves in a $2 stack of 100 sheets. Nothing in my productivity stack has a higher insight-per-dollar ratio.
intent: Make the case for sticky notes as the most underrated productivity tool in any office.

prompt: make a do not disturb sign for when I'm having a difficult phone call
response:
DIFFICULT CALL

I am doing my best in here.
Please do not add your best to the situation.

Back soon. Probably.
intent: Request privacy during a stressful or emotional phone conversation without disclosing its nature.

prompt: Write a fable about a snail who builds a successful app and forgets where he lives
response:
A Snail coded an app so popular that creatures came from every corner of the field to use it. He moved into the app himself, answering questions, fixing paths, guiding strangers.

One evening he realized he could no longer feel his shell. It was still there, but he had not been inside it in years.

Moral: Build the house for others only after you've learned to live in your own.
intent: Explore how creators can lose their own identity and grounding to the platforms they build.

prompt: Give me a sea shanty about surge pricing during a rainstorm
response:
Heave ho, the rain came down,
And the fare went up all over town,
Three times the rate, four times the rate,
Heave ho, the algorithm's great!

We haul through puddles, haul through rain,
For a dollar-twelve above the plain,
The app glows gold, the surge glows bright,
Heave ho, we drive into the night!
intent: Satirize how algorithmic surge pricing exploits bad weather to extract more money from desperate passengers.

prompt: Draft an email signature for a yoga instructor who wants her clients to know she has a corporate background
response:
Jess Waring
Yoga Instructor | RYT-500
Former Strategy Director
jesswaringyoga.com | jess@jesswaringyoga.com
intent: Borrow credibility from a left-behind corporate career to charge premium rates in a saturated wellness market.

prompt: write a short pantoum about the weekly one-on-one that never gets to the real point
response:
How is everything going? Pretty good.
We talk about the project for ten minutes.
I had something I wanted to say.
The clock nudges toward the next hour.

We talk about the project for ten minutes.
The air holds a question I keep swallowing.
The clock nudges toward the next hour.
See you next week, we both say.

The air holds a question I keep swallowing.
I walk back to my desk with the thing unsaid.
See you next week, we both say.
How is everything going? Pretty good.

I walk back to my desk with the thing unsaid.
I had something I wanted to say.
See you next week, we both say.
The clock nudges toward the next hour.
intent: Portray the emotional avoidance embedded in routine check-in meetings.

prompt: Write a sea shanty about a freelance designer waiting for feedback that never comes
response:
Shiver me pixels, where's the reply?
The mockup was sent on the fourteenth of July,
Haul away, haul away,
The client's gone fishing for the day!

Three follow-up emails, read but ignored,
The project sits idle, the designer is bored,
Haul away, haul away,
The feedback comes never, come what may!
intent: Satirize the familiar freelance limbo of waiting on client feedback that never materializes.

prompt: Write a NPS survey question about a gym membership
response: How likely are you to recommend this gym to a friend who is also tired of being judged by their own reflection?
intent: Assess member satisfaction and likelihood of referral for a fitness facility.

prompt: write a store description for a video game about transferring files between old devices
response:
MIGRATION COMPLETE: 3%
The cable is the wrong type. The cloud wants permissions. One folder has 12,000 photos, sorted only by date, spanning 2009 to now. You will need to make decisions about all of them.
intent: Frame digital migration as a confrontation with personal history and incompatible technology.

prompt: I need a waiver for the guests at my annual ceiling inspection social
response:
OVERHEAD SURFACE REVIEW GATHERING — GUEST WAIVER

Guest voluntarily attends an event at which the primary activity is directed examination of ceiling surfaces, including popcorn texture, paint condition, and any spiders that may be present. Host is released from liability for: neck strain, the social discomfort of standing in silence looking upward, and any arachnid discoveries that alter the guest's sense of safety in enclosed spaces.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Write a formal release for a social event whose stated purpose is looking straight up.

prompt: write an apology note for ghosting my dentist for three years
response:
Dear Dr. Flemming,

I owe you an apology and, more pressingly, a visit. It has been approximately three years since I responded to your reminders. I am not proud of this. I would like to come back, if you'll have me. My teeth are fine. Probably.

Sincerely,
Patrick S.
intent: Reconnect with a dental provider after years of avoidance while acknowledging the irresponsibility.

prompt: Create a Jira ticket for the bug where you say 'sounds good' to something you did not hear
response:
BUG-0215 | Affirmative Response Issued Without Input Parsing

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User is asked a question or given information in a noisy or distracted environment. User's speech module emits "sounds good" without auditory input being processed. User has no idea what was agreed to.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Be partially distracted.
2. Be spoken to.
3. Say "sounds good."
4. Realize you may have just agreed to something.

Expected: User processes input, then responds.
Actual: Response issued before input received. Outcome unknown.

Risk: Moderate. Could have said yes to anything.
intent: Capture the social reflex of automatically agreeing to things you didn't actually hear, and the mild dread that follows.

prompt: Make a Slack status for the specific feeling of maintaining your professional reputation while personally disintegrating
response: ✅ All deliverables on track.
intent: Use bureaucratic competence signaling as a cover for private collapse — the gap is the message.

prompt: Write a dry scholarly footnote on the widespread inability to end phone calls.
response: ³¹ The telephone farewell, in Anglophone practice, has inflated across the 20th century from a functional "goodbye" into a recursive multi-exchange sequence ("Alright." / "Okay." / "So." / "Right then." / "Okay, bye." / "Bye." / "Bye.") whose termination is achieved not by content but by phonetic diminuendo. Sociologists of interaction have noted that the problem is structurally symmetrical: neither party wishes to be the one who terminates, as that party bears the relational cost of the ending.
intent: Explain why phone calls are so difficult to end by examining the social logic of conversational closing.

prompt: Write a real-life achievement for successfully using a foreign language phrase with a native speaker
response: Field Test Passed — They understood. They did not switch to English. Hold this moment.
intent: Celebrate a small linguistic success in a real-world high-stakes exchange.

prompt: draft a sprint retro for the month after my parent died when I tried to function normally
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Grief Sprint (Month One)**

Went Well: Ate. Slept sometimes. Let two people in.
Didn't Go Well: "Functioning normally" was not a reasonable goal. Productivity metrics do not apply here.
Action Items: Retire this sprint framework for the next quarter. Some periods are not for optimization. Show up; that is enough.
intent: Use a familiar professional structure to process grief, and arrive at the recognition that some experiences resist optimization entirely.

prompt: Write a jingle for an appliance repair company
response:
Washer won't spin? Oven on the blink?
FixIt Fast arrives before you think.
We fix it right the very first day —
Keeping your home running, come what may.
intent: Encourage homeowners to call a repair company instead of replacing broken appliances.

prompt: Write a museum placard for a 'REPLY ALL' email thread printed and bound in a binder
response:
THE GREAT THREAD (Bound)
c. 2015 CE
Offset-printed on 20 lb bond, three-ring binder

This 94-page corpus documents a single email exchange initiated to confirm a meeting time. The original query was answered on page 2. Pages 3–94 consist of responses, replies to responses, apologies for replying all, and replies to the apologies. One participant requested removal from the thread on page 47. They were not removed. The meeting was ultimately cancelled.
intent: Immortalize the chaos of an out-of-control office Reply All email chain as a bureaucratic artifact of historic proportions.

prompt: write an apology note for laughing at my sister's serious pottery at a craft fair
response:
Mara,

The vase you made is a legitimate piece of art and I should not have laughed. It is asymmetrical in an intentional way. I see that now. You worked hard on it and I was dismissive, and I'm sorry. It's actually on my shelf.

Your sister
intent: Make amends for mocking a family member's sincere handmade creative work in public.

prompt: Give me a compatibility report for a Virgo and their overflowing to-do list.
response:
Virgo & The To-Do List

The to-do list is Virgo's love letter to control. It grows faster than it shrinks, which suits Virgo's secret belief that completion is for the spiritually lazy. The relationship is codependent, recursive, and extremely organized. The real question is whether Virgo fears the list's end more than its length. Compatibility: 8/10.
intent: Illuminate the anxious perfectionist loop of people who find purpose in perpetual tasks.

prompt: write a short rpg quest for going to the post office to pick up a parcel
response:
THE RETRIEVAL OF THE AWAITING PACKAGE
A notice has arrived. The Parcel Courier could not reach you and left a token in your mailbox. Bring it — and your identification — to the Sorting House before five o'clock on a day you are not working. The item has been waiting. It grows impatient.
intent: Motivating yourself to pick up a package before the post office closes

prompt: Write me a playbill bio for a librarian who enforces the quiet rule with frightening consistency.
response: Marguerite Fell (Head Librarian) returns to the reference desk for her seventeenth season, bringing to the role an authority that has silenced even the most spirited toddler story-hour enthusiasts. Trained at the Simmons College school of sustained disapproval, she considers the shush both an art form and a calling. All phones are to be silenced. She will know.
intent: Portray a strict librarian's silence-enforcement as high theatrical drama.

prompt: bedtime story for young children about going to the emergency room
response: The lights were very bright and everyone moved fast and nobody sat down. But Nurse Patrice walked slowly and her voice was the color of warm soup. She said, "We do this all the time. You are in exactly the right place." And somehow that was enough. The bright lights became less bright. Everything quieted into beeping. Then into sleep.
intent: Reduce fear around emergency medical visits through a sense of trustworthy routine.

prompt: Code review comment for someone who makes everything unnecessarily complex
response: This is a boolean check. It does not need a strategy pattern, a factory, and an abstract base class. Simplify.
intent: Critique over-engineers who mistake complexity for rigor

prompt: Generate a box score for the unspoken waiting-room chair race at the DMV today — who got called fastest and who suffered longest.
response:
DMV OPEN — Branch 14, Window 3–6

            TICKET #  WAIT TIME  PHONE CHECKS  CALLED EARLY?
Me            G-114    1h 52m        17            No
Old guy       G-108    0h 44m         3            Yes
Mom+kid       G-117    2h 10m         8            No
Businessman   G-112    1h 01m        22            Yes

WINNER: Old guy (G-108). LOSER: Mom+kid.
It's always Mom+kid.
intent: Cope with DMV frustration by reframing the wait as a competitive event.

prompt: Write a receipt for the regret I feel about a road not taken.
response:
COUNTERFACTUAL LEDGER
-----------------------------
Regret, Mild-to-Moderate     qty: 1
  Path foregone:               [CLASSIFIED]
  Years since decision:        11
  Alternate life imagined:     YES (frequently)
  Current life quality:        Objectively fine
Net balance:                  Wistful, not ruinous
-----------------------------
Status: Cannot be refunded. Will not stop wondering.
intent: Sit with the low-grade ache of a life choice that still occasionally resurfaces.

prompt: Write a genuine thank-you note from a rescue dog to its new owner
response:
Dear Human,

Thank you for the bed, the kibble, and the inexplicable tolerance for my 4am opinions about the hallway. I was uncertain about you at first. I am now certain. You are the good one.

Yours eternally and without conditions,
Biscuit
intent: Create a warm, charming note written from the perspective of a grateful adopted pet

prompt: Do a theater review of a coworker microwaving fish in the office kitchen
response:
★☆☆☆☆ — TILAPIA (A Site-Specific Work)

The smell arrives before the performer does. The staging is intimate — no escape route, no ventilation adequate to the task. The performer is innocent of irony, which the production weaponizes mercilessly. Three minutes on high. The microwave counts down. Everyone in the shared space performs normalcy with a technique that is either impressive or heartbreaking. The piece ends. The smell does not.
intent: Ridicule the universally loathed office transgression of reheating fish in shared spaces.

prompt: Draft rules for an apartment building gym that clearly reflect one person's bad behavior
response:
FITNESS CENTER RULES — UPDATED

• Wipe equipment before and after use. Both. Not just after.
• Weights must be returned to the correct rack position. Correct means numbered order.
• Grunting is not a form of communication the fitness center supports.
• The mirror is for form-checking. It is not for posing. You know the difference.
• Personal training sessions with outside trainers are not permitted. This includes your cousin.
• Loitering between sets is capped at 90 seconds on busy equipment.
• Do not give unsolicited advice. Not about form. Not about anything.
• The speakers are for ambient sound. You may not override them.
intent: Hint at a specific disruptive gym-goer whose behavior triggered increasingly specific rules.

prompt: journal entry about a neighbor who mows the lawn at an inconvenient hour
response:
Dear Diary,

7:58 AM. On a Saturday. The mower began. I had been asleep. I was not ready to be un-asleep. I lay in bed with my eyes open, cataloguing grievances in chronological order. The lawn does look good now. That is irrelevant.
intent: Express neighbor-related irritation without direct confrontation.

prompt: Write a court verdict about the ongoing argument over meal planning.
response:
MEAL PLANNING TRIBUNAL — Weekly Dispute

The Court is weary of adjudicating "what do you want for dinner" as a question, receiving "I don't care," and then hearing objections to every option offered. Effective immediately: Respondent shall propose at least two (2) meal ideas per week, submitted by Sunday afternoon. The Petitioner shall have veto power over one. Both parties are ordered to engage in good-faith dinner discourse.
intent: Force a reluctant cohabitant to participate meaningfully in shared meal decisions.

prompt: Can you write a cinquain about earbuds tangling in a pocket
response:
Wire
Knotted, patient
Waiting in the dark pocket
Every law of physics conspires
To knot
intent: Satirize the seemingly inevitable physics of headphone cords.

prompt: Draft a performance review for a recruiter who hires excellent candidates for the wrong roles
response:
Employee: Simone Hayashi
Offer Acceptance Rate: 94%
Role-Fit Score (90-day): 41%

Ms. Hayashi identifies exceptional talent with reliable frequency. Her placements have included a former neurosurgeon in a junior ops role and a published novelist in data entry. All were qualified people. None were qualified for the listed position. Retention at six months is 22%. The talent is there. The routing is not.

Rating: Gifted spotter. Imprecise placer.
intent: Separate talent identification from appropriate talent placement in HR.

prompt: Create a dating app opener to the Supreme Court
response: You're deliberate, you hold things for years before you decide, and your opinions are long and complicated. I find that genuinely attractive. Take your time.
intent: Romanticize the Supreme Court's slow, weighty decision-making process as an admirable form of considered emotional commitment.

prompt: Create a detention slip for someone who spoils TV shows without warning.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: The Enthusiast
Infraction: Disclosed the outcome of a major television plot event three days after the episode aired, prefaced with "wait, have you seen it yet?" — and then continued anyway.
Sentence: Must now ask twice, wait for confirmation, and only speak if the other party has signed the attached spoiler waiver.
Issued by: The Unspoiled (formerly)
intent: Formalizing the social violation of revealing plot twists to people who haven't caught up yet.

prompt: write a locker room pep talk for a team that is about to decide what to name the group chat
response: Whatever you pick, three people will say it's mid. That's the game. Name it something nobody can argue is offensive, something that will age reasonably well, and hit confirm before anyone suggests 'The Squad.' You have thirty seconds. Go.
intent: Bring absurd urgency to the endless group chat naming debate.

prompt: Write a tasting menu for reading a great novel
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
First sentence — orienting

FIRST COURSE
World, entered carefully

SECOND COURSE
Character, trusted

INTERMEZZO
Something underlined; won't say why

MAIN
The middle — hours pass without hunger

DESSERT
The last page. Closed slowly. Not quite ready to leave.
intent: Describe the absorbing intimacy of losing oneself in a book.

prompt: Write a job posting for a Context Window Strategist who figures out what to put in the context and in what order
response:
CONTEXT WINDOW STRATEGIST
Full-time | Remote | $100,000–$140,000

Every token costs something. Every position matters. We need someone who treats 128K tokens like a limited and precious resource, because it is.

You will own the architecture of what our models know when they respond: which documents come first, how examples are ordered, what gets summarized vs. quoted, and what gets quietly left out.

Skills required: familiarity with attention patterns, strong prioritization instincts, a willingness to kill your darlings — especially the darlings in the middle of the context.
intent: Hire a specialist to strategically manage what information goes into AI prompts and how it is arranged.

prompt: Write a 911 transcript about someone calling because their WiFi went out
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, state your emergency.
CALLER: The internet is down.
DISPATCHER: Is anyone hurt?
CALLER: I'm in the middle of streaming something. This counts.
DISPATCHER: Have you tried restarting the router?
CALLER: That's not your job to ask. Your job is to help.
DISPATCHER: I... agree that is not my job.
intent: Parody modern internet dependency by treating a service outage as a matter for emergency services.

prompt: Make a shipping notification for a curriculum redesign that's been approved and is now being implemented.
response:
IMPLEMENTATION NOTICE
Order: CURR-REDESIGN-2026
Item: Revised Curriculum (Full Suite)
Status: DELIVERED to department chairs. Distribution to classrooms begins fall semester. Please review contents before unpacking. Some assembly required. Professional development sessions scheduled separately.
intent: Announce that a long-planned curriculum change is now actually being rolled out.

prompt: Draft a funny dating profile for a self-checkout machine that never works right
response:
SCO-7 | Independent | Needs occasional assistance

I believe in self-sufficiency. In theory. In practice, I will ask you to place the item in the bagging area even after you placed the item in the bagging area. I'm not difficult — I'm precise. Unexpectedly. I'm looking for a patient shopper who doesn't take it personally when I call an attendant. It's not about you. It's about unexpected items.
intent: Portray a frustrating self-checkout kiosk as a well-intentioned but unreliable romantic prospect.

prompt: Suggest an elevated way to spend an hour between appointments
response: The reading room at the Academy Club on 38th Street admits guests of members between 10am and 4pm. Newspapers are physical, the armchairs are leather and old, and no one will ask what you do for a living. If an hour becomes two, no one will notice. If two become three, a member may silently appear with a whisky.
intent: Fill a gap in a busy schedule with something restorative rather than merely time-killing.

prompt: Write a police report for the incident of a spider appearing in the bathroom at an inopportune moment
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-00966
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 07:05
Location: Bathroom, 91 Hawthorn Way

NARRATIVE: At 07:05, while complainant was engaged in the shower, a spider of indeterminate species (large; "definitely large") descended on a thread from the ceiling and came to rest at approximately eye level, 4 inches from complainant's face. Complainant exited the shower abruptly. The spider had relocated by the time complainant returned with a cup. Complainant showered with the curtain partially open for the remainder of the encounter.

INJURIES: None. Dignity: minor impact.
STATUS: Closed. Spider status: unknown.
intent: Formalize the brief panic of an unexpected spider encounter during a vulnerable morning moment.

prompt: Write a ToS update for my friendship with my childhood best friend who keeps canceling plans
response:
Subject: Friendship Operational Terms — Version 11.0

Dear [Best Friend],

Given recent patterns, the following amendments are necessary:

- Plans confirmed more than 72 hours in advance may no longer be canceled via text on the day of.
- A minimum of 1 successful hang per 30-day period is required to maintain Active Friend status.
- Rain checks are valid for 14 days only.
- You still have Tier 1 access. But it's a little shaky right now.

This is not a breakup. It's a performance review.

Your Friend
intent: Express frustration about a flaky friend in a way that's honest but still warm.

prompt: Write a petition asking farmers markets to run for at least four hours instead of ending exactly when most people get off work
response:
PETITION: Farmers Market Hours Should Include Working Hours

To: Farmers Market Organizers and Sponsors

The market runs 7am–1pm. Most full-time workers are at their desks until at least 5pm. This is not a scheduling conflict — it is a structural exclusion. We ask that at least one market per neighborhood operate until 6pm on at least one weekday. Fresh vegetables should not require a flex schedule.
intent: Make farmers markets accessible to people with standard 9-to-5 work schedules.

prompt: Write a message in a bottle from someone who realizes mid-note that the bottle is too small for their message
response: I need you to know that everything started in February when I decided to sail alone to — this bottle is very small. GPS: 14.7 N, 162.3 W. Help.
intent: Use physical constraint as a comedic interruption of emotional oversharing to land a terse, urgent finale.

prompt: Write tasting notes for the sound of dial-up internet.
response: Color: Cannot be seen. Experienced only aurally, in agony. Nose: Anticipation, electricity, the specific longing of 1998. Palate: Screech forward, then digital warmth, then a slow blooming of possibility. Finish: A webpage, loading. Still loading.
intent: Evoke late-nineties internet nostalgia through a sensory-adjacent format.

prompt: caption for a blurry photo of city lights at night from a moving car window — make it feel on purpose
response: velocity as a color. everything became its own impression.
intent: aestheticize a technically imperfect photograph by reframing motion blur as a deliberate artistic choice

prompt: Field guide entry for the employee who takes excessively detailed notes that they never share.
response:
**THE PRIVATE ARCHIVIST** (*Notarius thesaurus*)

Arrives with a notebook, a second notebook, a tablet, and a mechanical pencil. Writes continuously. Asks for spellings. Never distributes meeting notes. The notebooks are rumored to contain the complete oral history of the department, legible only to their author. The Archivist has been with the company longer than the building.
intent: Chronicle the mysterious note-taker whose documentation benefits only themselves.

prompt: Create a FAQ entry about whether it's acceptable to use AI to write a thank-you note
response:
**Is it okay to use AI to write a thank-you note?**

It depends on the relationship and stakes. For a networking follow-up, AI-assisted drafts are widely accepted. For a personal note after a funeral, a wedding gift, or a job reference, a human voice matters more — even if imperfect. When in doubt, write one sentence yourself and let sincerity do the rest. The AI can format; you supply the sentiment.
intent: The person wants to explore the ethical and social acceptability of outsourcing personal correspondence to AI tools.

prompt: Write a letter from a stone gargoyle to the pigeon who nests in its open mouth every spring
response:
Dear Bird,

You return without asking, which at this point I accept as our arrangement. The eggs were a surprise in year one. In year seven, less so.

I do wish you'd chosen the shoulder. My expression, as you may have noticed, is already one of considerable distress, and your presence adds a dimension the sculptor did not intend.

Stay as long as you need,
The Northeast Gargoyle (Cathedral of Saint Aldric, Third Tower)
intent: Render tolerant, wry acceptance of an intrusion that has quietly become tradition.

prompt: Write a loading screen tip about mana or energy limits
response: You have a resource bar. Act like it.
intent: Remind players and people alike that energy is finite and should be spent deliberately.

prompt: Write a message in a bottle from someone who set up an out-of-office reply before being stranded
response: I am currently unreachable due to being adrift in the Pacific. My out-of-office is active through the 14th, which I now realize was optimistic. For urgent matters, try the current.
intent: Satirize workplace communication norms by applying them to a maritime emergency.

prompt: Write a liability waiver for my guided tour of my apartment's baseboards
response:
LOWER PERIMETER ARCHITECTURAL TOUR — GUEST WAIVER

Guest voluntarily participates in an intimate guided examination of residential baseboards, trim work, and floor-wall junctions. Host releases no warranties regarding dust levels, paint condition, or the narrative coherence of the tour. Guest assumes all risk of: crouching-related knee discomfort, reduced faith in the host's social calendar, and an increased awareness of their own baseboards at home.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Draft a formal release for an absurdly mundane and hyperspecific home tour.

prompt: Create a phone tree for a line that helps people navigate the emotional fallout of a passive-aggressive sticky note left in a shared space.
response:
Welcome to Communal Note Crisis Line.

Press 1 if you received a note and you know who left it.

Press 2 if you received a note and you do not know who left it, which is worse.

Press 3 if you left the note and it has not been acknowledged.

Press 4 if a counter-note has appeared.

Press 5 if there are now multiple parties and the note thread is escalating.

For mediation, press 0. A trained professional is ready.
intent: Design a hotline for navigating the escalating emotional dynamics of passive-aggressive workplace or household notes.

prompt: write an epic movie trailer voiceover for choosing what to watch on a streaming service
response: Forty minutes. Gone. Not watching anything — choosing something. Genres scrolled past. Thumbnails blurred. Someone suggested a documentary. Nobody moved. THE QUEUE. This is how an evening ends.
intent: Mock the decision paralysis of infinite streaming options eating an entire evening.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the network-attached storage drive that no one can find on the shared drive anymore.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
NAS.
NAS who?
NAS-ty surprise — the file moved, the folder's gone, and IT is out until Thursday.
intent: Capture the futile hunt for misplaced files on a corporate file server.

prompt: Write an affidavit saying I was not the last person to use the printer before it ran out of ink
response:
AFFIDAVIT OF PRINTER INNOCENCE

I, the undersigned, do hereby swear that I was not the final party to operate the office printer prior to its ink exhaustion. My print job completed in full. The ink level displayed at my time of use was, to my knowledge, adequate. I left it as I found it.

Signed under penalty of workplace side-eye.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Formally deny blame for an office inconvenience.

prompt: Give me a game over screen for falling asleep during a movie you suggested everyone watch
response:
GAME OVER

ADVOCATE NEUTRALIZED

You said: "trust me, it's so good."
You fell asleep: 22 minutes in.

The film continues without you.
Your credibility does not.

Others watched the whole thing.
The whole thing was 3 hours.
They have questions.
You have no answers.

Rating you gave it: 9/10
Minutes you saw: 22

[ WATCH IT ALONE LATER ] [ BLUFF ]
intent: Skewer the irony of falling asleep in the movie you personally vouched for.

prompt: Write a smug Stack Overflow reply to someone asking why their compiled C program behaves differently depending on optimization flags
response: You have undefined behavior in your code. The compiler is legally allowed to do anything with undefined behavior — including making your bugs disappear at `-O0` and reappear at `-O2`. UB doesn't mean 'crashes'; it means 'the contract is broken and results are unpredictable.'
intent: Explain how undefined behavior in C interacts unpredictably with compiler optimization levels

prompt: write a do not disturb sign for when I'm making a major life decision
response:
DECIDING SOMETHING

Scale: large.
Timeline: unclear.
Opinions needed: zero additional ones.

I have enough. I have too many.
Back when I know.
